title: Ep 88: Unpacking DeepMind's Quest for SuperIntelligence with Demis Hassabis' Biographer

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Demis has a Nobel Prize.
Sam didn't finish his first degree.
Therefore, Demis doesn't take Sam very seriously.

paragraphs.zh: Demis 得过 Nobel Prize，而 Sam 连他的第一个学位都没有读完。因此，Demis 并不会太把 Sam 当回事。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Do you think he feels like or or will have to become more of a public figure than he is today?

paragraphs.zh: 你觉得，相比今天，他是否会想要——或者说将不得不——变得更像一个公众人物？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Demis, in some ways, is too sensible.

paragraphs.zh: 从某种意义上说，Demis 有点过于理性稳重了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: What was the moment, I guess, your view of him kind of shifted the most?

paragraphs.zh: 我想问的是，大概在什么时刻，你对他的看法发生了最大转变？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: He would sometimes erupt in these conversations I was having, and he would start banging the table and saying, maybe if we approach science the right way, we understand more about nature, we will be getting closer to something that we could perhaps call God.

paragraphs.zh: 有时在我与他进行这些谈话时，他会突然激动起来，开始拍桌子，说，也许如果我们以正确的方式接近科学，更理解自然，我们就会更接近某种也许可以称之为 God 的东西。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Sebastian Malaby spent over thirty hours with DeepMind CEO and cofounder, Demis Esabas, in preparation to write his book, The Infinity Machine.
I got to sit down with Sebastian to get all his reflections on this experience.

It was fascinating to talk about some of the stories that Sebastian reported on in this book, including the fact that Reid Hoffman offered a billion dollars to Demis to spin DeepMind out of Google at some point.

We talked about Demis' relationship with Elon and how that's evolved over the years, as well as what he thinks of Sam, Dario, and the general relationship between all the AI leaders in the space today.
And we hit on Sebastian's reflections on Demis, what motivates him, what his blind spots are.

paragraphs.zh: 为了写他的书 The Infinity Machine，Sebastian Malaby 花了三十多个小时与 DeepMind 的 CEO 兼联合创始人 Demis Esabas 相处交流。我也有机会和 Sebastian 坐下来聊聊，听他谈这段经历的所有感想。这次谈话非常精彩，我们聊到了一些 Sebastian 在这本书中报道的故事，其中包括这样一个事实：Reid Hoffman 曾在某个时候向 Demis 开价 10 亿美元，想让他把 DeepMind 从 Google 分拆出来。我们还谈到了 Demis 与 Elon 的关系，以及这些年来这种关系如何演变；也谈到了他对 Sam、Dario 的看法，以及当下这个领域里各位 AI 领袖之间的大体关系。我们也谈到了 Sebastian 对 Demis 的观察：是什么在驱动他，他的盲点又是什么。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Just a fascinating conversation with someone who's spent unprecedented time with someone who's really, at the cutting edge of AI today.
I think folks will really enjoy Sebastian's candid takes here.
Without further ado, here he is.
Well, Sebastian, thanks so much for coming on the podcast.
Really appreciate it.

paragraphs.zh: 这真是一场非常精彩的对话——对方是一个与当今真正处在 AI 前沿的人相处了前所未有之久的人。我想大家一定会很喜欢 Sebastian 在这里这些坦率直接的看法。事不宜迟，让我们请出他。Sebastian，非常感谢你来上 podcast，真的非常感激。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Thank you.
It's great to be with you.

paragraphs.zh: 谢谢。很高兴和你一起聊。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I feel like the Infinity Machine and your kind of story of DeepMind and Demis Esabas has captivated a bunch of folks in the AI world.
I know that I inhaled this book.
I think I finished it in, like, twenty four hours.
It's just a a gripping story, and you got this just incredible access to Demis.
I think, you know, the sitting in this pub for for multi hours at a time, talking about all sorts of things.

paragraphs.zh: 我感觉《The Infinity Machine》，以及你讲述 DeepMind 和 Demis Esabas 的这类故事，已经深深吸引了 AI 圈里的很多人。我知道我自己就是一口气读完了这本书。我想我大概在二十四小时内就读完了。这个故事非常扣人心弦，而你又获得了对 Demis 极其罕见、惊人的接触机会。我想，比如你们一次次在那家 pub 里一坐就是好几个小时，聊各种各样的事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Just just a fascinating, you know, opportunity and window into to definitely one of the most interesting people and and companies in this AI race today.

And so appreciate you writing the book and appreciate you coming on the podcast to talk about So our goal today is to, you know, use your reporting to understand the people, decisions, and dynamics that shape the AI race that we're living through.

And so maybe to start, I feel like one of the interesting themes in your book is whether this race that we have today between the labs was inevitable or could have gone differently, if different players or decisions had had been done.
I guess I'm curious.
After doing this work, do you think there was another path, or or was this kind of inevitable?

paragraphs.zh: 这真的是一个非常迷人的机会，也是一个窗口，让人得以窥见当下这场 AI 竞赛中最有意思的人物和公司之一。所以很感谢你写了这本书，也感谢你来到这个 podcast 来聊这些。我们今天的目标，是借助你的报道来理解塑造我们当下所处这场 AI 竞赛的人物、决策和动态。所以也许先从这里开始：我觉得你书里一个很有意思的主题，是我们今天看到的这种 labs 之间的竞赛究竟是不可避免的，还是本来可能走向另一条路——如果当时由不同的参与者做出不同的决定的话。我很好奇，在做完这些工作之后，你觉得当时是否存在另一条路径，还是说这一切其实某种程度上是注定会发生的？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You know, I think it was inevitable.
I think when you have this sort of supremely strong technology, there's gonna be multiple labs in multiple countries that are just desperate to try and build it.
And we know the China stack, you know, is pretty strong.
And so despite the lack of semiconductors, they were gonna have a go at it and they are actually doing pretty well.
And then clearly in The US, and then in a few other countries, you've got the Austral in France, Coher in Canada.

paragraphs.zh: 你知道，我觉得这是不可避免的。我认为，当你面对这样一种极其强大的技术时，多个国家里的多个 labs 一定都会拼命想把它造出来。我们也知道，中国这套 stack 相当强。所以尽管缺少 semiconductors，他们也一定会去尝试，而事实上他们现在做得也相当不错。然后显然在 The US，以及其他少数几个国家里，你还有 Austral（法国）、Coher（加拿大）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You know, there was bound to be many players just as it's the technology is too sweet for it to be only interesting to one team.
What's strange about this whole debate though is that's not how people saw it ex ante, that, you know, when Demis was starting DeepMind, he really hoped that he could avoid the race dynamic.
It seems naive in retrospect, but that's what he hoped.

paragraphs.zh: 可以肯定会有很多参与者，因为这项技术的吸引力太大了，不可能只让一个团队感兴趣。不过，这整场讨论里真正奇怪的地方在于，人们事前并不是这样看的。Demis 当初创办 DeepMind 的时候，确实希望自己能够避开这种竞赛动态。现在回头看，这似乎有点天真，但那的确是他当时的希望。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: One really compelling anecdote you had was I I believe Demis in his interviews would say, you know, he he was interviewing candidate, and he'd say, look.
At some point, we may be really close to AGI, and we'll go, fly to a bunker and and and figure this all out as kind of like one team.
Would you be willing to, you know, get on that flight and and and do that?
You know, think certainly as part of the the founding origin of Anthropic is also this belief like, hey.
We need to be the team that, like, gets you know, as we get to the precipice of AGI, we're the ones figuring this out.

paragraphs.zh: 你书里有一个特别有说服力的轶事。我记得 Demis 在采访中会对候选人说：你看，到了某个时候，我们可能会非常接近 AGI（通用人工智能），到那时我们会飞去一个 bunker，然后像一个团队那样把这一切彻底搞清楚。你愿不愿意登上那班飞机，去做这件事？我想，很明显，Anthropic 创立时的起源之一也有这种信念：就是，嘿，我们需要成为那个团队——当我们走到 AGI 的悬崖边时，由我们来把这件事弄明白。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: You know, clearly, a a key part of starting all these companies.
Do you think these folks, I mean, particularly, Demis, like, do they still believe this, or or how is their kind of thinking evolved having seen the way things have played out these past years?

paragraphs.zh: 你知道，这显然是创办所有这些公司时一个关键组成部分。你觉得这些人——尤其是 Demis——他们现在还相信这个吗？还是说，在看到过去这些年事情实际是如何发展之后，他们的想法已经发生了变化？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: No. I think Demis has swung from one extreme to the other.
You know, he began by thinking there could be a singleton scenario, just one lab.
And by that, he really meant deep mind in himself.

To the opposite extreme where he now sees that there's a very crowded field and therefore that it's almost pointless for one lab to pursue safety by itself because if one lab is safe and then the other ones aren't, it doesn't make the world safer.

paragraphs.zh: 不，我觉得 Demis 已经从一个极端摆到了另一个极端。你知道，他一开始认为可能会出现 singleton scenario（单一主体情境），也就是只有一个 lab。而说到底，他所谓的这个 lab，其实就是指 DeepMind 和他自己。现在则走到了相反的极端：他看到的是一个极其拥挤的竞争场，因此他认为，单靠一个 lab 自己去追求 safety（安全）几乎没有意义，因为如果一个 lab 是安全的，而其他 labs 不是，那并不会让世界变得更安全。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So he really has shifted to seeing this as a collective action problem that only a government can solve.

paragraphs.zh: 所以他确实已经转而把这看作一个 collective action problem（集体行动问题），而这种问题只有政府才能解决。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Because one thing I I thought was really compelling for your book was, obviously, there was this early AI safety summit that Demis and the and the team put together, and they're sharing, you know, the progress they've made and and why they're worried about it.
And, know, you some people in the room like Reid Hoffman and Elon Musk take that information, and then they're like, oh, this technology really is starting to work.
We should ourselves potentially do something around that.
You know, do you think, obviously, to to get safety to work going forward, you need these different companies to to agree to share things and and collaborate.
I imagine that left a bad taste in in in Demis' mouth.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得你书里有一点特别有说服力：显然，当时 Demis 和团队组织了那场早期的 AI safety（AI 安全）峰会，他们在会上分享了自己取得的进展，以及他们为什么对此感到担忧。然后，房间里像 Reid Hoffman 和 Elon Musk 这样的一些人听到这些信息后，就会想，哦，这项技术看来真的开始起作用了，我们自己或许也该围绕这件事做点什么。你觉得，显然，如果今后真要把安全工作做好，就需要这些不同公司同意分享信息并开展合作。我想那件事一定让 Demis 心里很不是滋味。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: How do you think he and and other kind of key players in this space think about, you know, potentially sharing what they're doing with with given that, you know, past experience?

paragraphs.zh: 你觉得，考虑到过去那样的经历，他以及这个领域其他一些关键人物，会如何看待与他人分享他们正在做的事情这件事？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
I mean, you're totally right.
That was in 2015, summer of twenty fifteen.
They have this summit SpaceX.
Elon Musk is hosting it, and the idea is he's gonna be brought into the tent by DeepMind.

paragraphs.zh: 对，我觉得你说得完全对。那是在 2015 年，2015 年夏天。他们在 SpaceX 办了这场峰会，是 Elon Musk 主办的，而当时的想法是，DeepMind 要把他拉进这个圈子里来。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: He's gonna be part of their efforts.
He's going to be, you know, chairing this sort of safety oversight board, And therefore, he wouldn't set up a competitor.
And, of course, at the end of 2015, he did set up a competitor, OpenAI.
And so that kind of really drove home the reality that we're gonna have a race.
And I think if you ask now, okay.

paragraphs.zh: 他会参与他们的工作，会担任某种 safety（安全）监督委员会的主席，这样一来，他就不会去创办一个竞争对手了。但当然，到了 2015 年底，他还是创办了一个竞争对手，OpenAI。所以这件事等于非常清楚地说明了一个现实：我们将会进入一场竞赛。我想如果你现在再问，好吧，

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So what about future collaboration?
I think from Tembus' point of view or from any of the lab leaders, frankly, you can't trust the other guys.
And therefore, the only way you get trust is if you have a government enforcer that comes along and say, look.
Here's the rules for everybody.
There's gonna be a level playing field.

paragraphs.zh: 那未来的合作怎么办？我认为，从 Tembus 的角度，或者坦率地说，从任何实验室负责人的角度来看，你没法信任别家。因此，唯一能够建立信任的方式，就是由政府监管者出面说，听着，这里是适用于所有人的规则。大家会处在一个公平的竞争环境中。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You're all gonna have to, you know, abide by some sort of safety, slowdown, you know, pretesting of models before you release them, all that stuff.
And you will have to do it.
And then the reaction, of course, will be, yeah.
But what about the guys in China?
And that's why, ultimately, I think this has to be a US China collaboration, you know, remote though that prospect may seem to many listeners right now.

paragraphs.zh: 你们都必须遵守某种 safety（安全）要求、减速措施，以及在发布模型之前进行 pretesting（预先测试）之类的规定，诸如此类。而且你们必须这么做。当然，接下来的反应就会是：那 China 的那些人怎么办？所以我最终认为，这必须是一个 US-China 协作，尽管对现在很多听众来说，这种前景可能显得非常遥远。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I mean, do you think, Demis and others, I think that's actually realistic?

I mean, obviously, there there's it you know, it's it's such a a pressing problem, but, you know, it doesn't seem like, folks have the most confidence in in governments and certainly, you know, inter government, collaboration across the world.
Like, it it intellectually makes sense that that is kind of the the clear way to solve this problem.
But do you think they they think there's actually a probability this this does happen successfully?

paragraphs.zh: 你的意思是，你觉得 Demis 和其他人真的认为这现实可行吗？我的意思是，显然，这确实是一个非常紧迫的问题，但看起来人们对政府、尤其是跨国政府协作，并没有太大的信心。从理性上说，这似乎确实是解决这个问题的明确路径，但你觉得他们是否真的认为，这件事有成功发生的可能性？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Well, you know, I think they ought to believe that it could happen.

And we we do have the Food and Drug Administration and people complain that it's slow and all that, but but it does, you know, have expert reviewers who look at clinical trials and say is this drug safe or not and can it be released.
And if you can do that for pharmaceuticals and you should do it, AI models that can be more destructive on a grander scale than a drug certainly need to be reviewed ex ante.
And I think in Britain actually you have an example of a government AI safety institute that's pretty effective, has some high class scientists.
I mean the chief scientist there is Geoffrey Irving who was at DeepMind originally did a lot of the post training on the early models.

paragraphs.zh: 嗯，我觉得他们应该相信这件事是可能发生的。我们确实有 Food and Drug Administration，虽然人们会抱怨它效率慢之类的问题，但它确实有专业审查人员来审阅 clinical trials（临床试验），并判断这种药物是否安全、能否上市。如果 pharmaceuticals（药品）可以这样做，而且本来也应该这样做，那么 AI 模型——它们造成的破坏规模可能比药物更大——当然更应该在事前接受审查。我还认为，Britain 其实就有一个例子：政府设立的 AI safety institute（AI 安全研究所）相当有效，也有一些一流科学家。比如那里的 chief scientist（首席科学家）是 Geoffrey Irving，他最初就在 DeepMind，曾参与早期模型的大量 post training（后训练）工作。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: He's a really serious researcher.
Was also at OpenAI by the way and a close collaborator of Darius.
So you've got serious people there and they have found vulnerabilities in systems in the past and quietly shared them with the private labs that have failed to find the same vulnerabilities.
So it shows that you can aggregate some technical expertise inside these public institutions because a certain number of folk feel motivated and public spirited enough to go work for the government.
So I don't think we should give up on that.

paragraphs.zh: 他是一位非常严肃认真的研究者。顺便说一句，他之前也在 OpenAI，而且还是 Darius 的亲密合作者。所以那边确实有很硬核的人，而且他们过去曾在系统中发现过漏洞，并且低调地把这些问题分享给那些自己没能发现同样漏洞的私营实验室。这说明，在这些公共机构内部，你是可以汇聚一定技术专长的，因为总会有一部分人出于动力和公共精神，愿意去政府工作。所以我不认为我们应该放弃这一点。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: From the outside, it's hard to tell.
You know, it it's almost like such a dispiriting story of, you know, Demis kinda being idealistic and then realizing this dynamic isn't gonna play out.

And so it's hard to tell, you know, I guess, reading between the lines in your book, whether he's kind of just resigned to this dynamic or is is found newfound hope in in, like, the the actual possibility of, you know, intergovernment collaboration?
Because, obviously, even the examples you give are just within a single country.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 从外部很难判断。你知道，这几乎像是一个让人泄气的故事：Demis 某种程度上一直很理想主义，后来又意识到这种局面不会按理想的方式发展。所以我想说，读你这本书时，很难判断他到底是某种程度上已经对这种局面认命了，还是说他重新对那种真正实现政府间协作的可能性燃起了希望？因为很明显，你举的那些例子其实都还只是发生在单一国家内部，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Certainly not at the scale of of of coordination across countries.

paragraphs.zh: 当然还远远谈不上那种跨国协调的规模。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
I mean, what he what he says, I mean, the very last line of my book is, I'm optimistic still, and that's a quote from Demis trying to, you know, persuade me.
I've been I'm pushing him in in in my interviews with him.
Are you really optimistic?
Because it seems like we've got a race dynamic, you know, in, of course, January 2025.

paragraphs.zh: 对。我的意思是，他自己说的话——我这本书的最后一句其实就是“我仍然乐观”，那是 Demis 的原话，他当时是在试图说服我。我在和他做采访时一直在追问他：你真的乐观吗？因为看起来我们显然已经处在一种竞赛动态里了，尤其是在 2025 年 1 月这个时间点上。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You have deep sea come out, and that really signals the advent of the Chinese labs into this space.
And, you know, I'm writing through 2025 into the end of twenty twenty five, and I'm saying, you still optimistic, Demet?
And he says, yes.
I'm optimistic still.
And I think the reason for that insistence apart from like, what what's he gonna say?

paragraphs.zh: 你看到 deep sea 出现了，而这确实标志着 Chinese labs 开始进入这个领域。然后我一路写到 2025 年、写到 2025 年底，我还在问他：你还是乐观的吗，Demet？他说，是的，我仍然乐观。我觉得他之所以如此坚持，除了说——不然他还能怎么说呢？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It's also that, you know, when something kind of dramatic happens, the world does coalesce and take action.
You know, if you look at COVID, the reaction to COVID, I mean, government on a national basis really did stuff that people didn't expect beforehand.
You know?
So in a crisis, people do react.
When you have something like, you know, trade where protectionism has a pretty slow and subtle effect on economic performance, you don't really get a political reaction to try and fix that.

paragraphs.zh: 还有一个原因是，当某种足够戏剧性的事情发生时，世界确实会凝聚起来并采取行动。你看 COVID，看看对 COVID 的反应，我的意思是，各国政府在国家层面上确实做了很多人事先想不到的事，对吧？所以在危机中，人们的确会作出反应。但像贸易这种事情，protectionism（保护主义）对经济表现的影响往往是很缓慢、很微妙的，于是你通常不会看到政治层面出现那种试图去修正它的反应。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And that's what we've seen in the last few years.

But when you when it if if you have a acute thing like mythos, an anthropics model comes out, you know, and people suddenly get worried, You see the US government pretty much do a one eighty from a less affair position to, oh, well, we better control this kind of position.
And I think that shows the shock effect is extremely important in determining whether you get government action or not.

paragraphs.zh: 而这正是我们过去几年所看到的情况。但如果你遇到的是某种尖锐、急性的事件，比如 mythos，Anthropic 的 model（模型）发布了，然后人们突然开始担心，你就会看到 US government 几乎来了个 one eighty（一百八十度转弯），从一种 less affair 的立场变成“哦，那我们最好还是得管一管”的立场。我认为这说明，shock effect（冲击效应）对于最终会不会出现政府行动，极其关键。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Switching gears, obviously, a key part of, you know, and through line through your your whole work is just the story of of DeepMind and its ultimate relationship with Google, and it's just an incredible story.
And so I guess, as I recall, you started this or you kinda got Demis to agree to this, like, right before ChatGPT.
And so, obviously, post AlphaFold and some really interesting things, but certainly the world, evolved in a bunch of interesting ways from the start of your project.
People talk about Demis and DeepMind all the time.

You know, before you published this book, but you've kinda done the research, what do you think the popular narrative got, like, most wrong about Demees, DeepMind, the way people would talk about, you know, the the two?

paragraphs.zh: 换个话题。显然，你整个作品里一个关键组成部分、也是贯穿始终的主线，就是 DeepMind 以及它最终与 Google 之间关系的故事，而这确实是一个非常惊人的故事。所以我想，按我的印象，你开始做这个项目、或者说你让 Demis 同意这个项目时，应该是在 ChatGPT 出现之前不久。于是很明显，那时已经有了 AlphaFold 和一些非常有意思的进展，但从你这个项目启动到后来，世界又以很多耐人寻味的方式发生了变化。人们一直都在谈论 Demis 和 DeepMind，在你出版这本书之前就是如此；但你算是真正做了深入研究的人，所以你觉得，大众叙事对于 Demis、DeepMind，以及人们通常如何谈论这两者，最大的误解到底是什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Well, the extraordinary thing to me is how people basically discounted Demees.
I mean, you know, I I've been interviewed a bunch of times since my book came out a few weeks ago and, you know, often people can't pronounce his name.
They say Demise and they say Hasabis instead of Hasabis.
They kind of barely know who he is.
You know my publisher Penguin Press was figuring out what to do the how to do the cover design.

paragraphs.zh: 嗯，对我来说最不同寻常的一点是，人们基本上都低估了 Demis。我的意思是，自从我的书几周前出版以来，我已经接受了不少采访，而你知道，人们经常连他的名字都不会念。他们会说成 Demise，还会把 Hasabis 念错成别的发音。他们几乎都不太知道他是谁。你知道，我的出版商 Penguin Press 当时还在琢磨封面设计该怎么做。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And they wanted to use his picture but at the same time they didn't think people would recognize him.
So you couldn't sell books based on some, you know, person that he recognized.
Right.

paragraphs.zh: 他们想用他的照片，但同时又觉得人们认不出他。所以你没法靠一个、你知道、别人都不认识的人来卖书。对吧。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: At this point, I assume Sam, Dario, they'd be fine for for a cover of a book.

paragraphs.zh: 到了现在，我觉得如果是 Sam、Dario，上书封面应该都没问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
Totally.

paragraphs.zh: 对，对，完全是这样。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Is it Elon.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 是 Elon 吗。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Elon, for sure.
And and Demis, you know, is the guy who founded the OG lab in 2010 way before anybody else really created the model that then OpenAI copied later.

I remember going to see Dario when I was doing my research and, you know, he said, yes, Demis was the original figure and he's got the whole AI for science space to himself which was true at the time.
I think it's less true now.
But so I think people just underestimated how important he he is.

paragraphs.zh: Elon 当然算。而且 Demis，你知道，是 2010 年创办那家 OG lab（元老级实验室）的人，远早于其他人，真正先做出了后来被 OpenAI 复制的那种模式。我记得我做调研时去见过 Dario，而你知道，他当时说，没错，Demis 才是最初的代表人物，而且整个 AI for science 领域几乎都是他一个人的天下——那在当时确实是事实。我觉得现在这种说法就没那么成立了。但所以我认为，人们就是低估了他到底有多重要。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: They underestimated Google as a company because they thought Innovator's Dilemma, they were too slow.
OpenAI went fast with a model with a with a chatbot.
And now they've got this massive brand effect, OpenAI does.
And so nobody can catch up.
That proved wrong.

paragraphs.zh: 他们也低估了 Google 这家公司，因为他们觉得这是典型的 Innovator’s Dilemma（创新者窘境），觉得它动作太慢。OpenAI 则靠一个 model（模型）、一个 chatbot（聊天机器人）快速推进。现在 OpenAI 还形成了巨大的品牌效应。所以大家就认为，谁都追不上了。结果证明这判断是错的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And you know, as of late twenty twenty five, the Google DeepMind models, Gemini three point o were better on the leaderboards than the adversaries.
Of course, since then we've seen a big anthropic surge.
But you know, the the point is I think people were too quick to crown OpenAI and Sam Altman as the winner and underestimated both Demis as a person and Google DeepMind as a company.

paragraphs.zh: 而且你知道，到了 2025 年末，Google DeepMind 的模型 Gemini 3.0 在各类 leaderboards（排行榜）上的表现已经超过了对手。当然，从那之后我们也看到了 Anthropic 的一波强势增长。但重点是，我认为人们太快就把 OpenAI 和 Sam Altman 捧成赢家了，同时低估了 Demis 这个人，也低估了 Google DeepMind 这家公司。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Is interesting, obviously, like, since you're reporting, you know, I guess in 2026, you've had these rise of these, you know, coding agents and, you know, it's certainly, you know, Anthropic and Cloud Code were the first there and OpenAI, you know, with with Codex was was right behind.
It does feel like Google continues to do, like, amazing science and, you know, they do you know, they always do well on the leaderboards.
But for whatever reason, it it struggles in, like, the zeitgeist and, like, actual usage on the product side.

If these two monumental moments were, you know, ChatGPT and consumer products and then ClaudeCode and and and the coding agents, Google doesn't really have a a super competitive product in either of those spaces just from a usage perspective.
Not the products themselves are are good.

paragraphs.zh: 这很有意思，显然，既然你是在报道，我想是到 2026 年，你已经看到这些 coding agents（编程 agent）的兴起，而且很明显，Anthropic 和 Cloud Code 是最早入场的，OpenAI 则凭借 Codex 紧随其后。感觉上，Google 还是持续在做了不起的科学研究，而且他们在各种排行榜上也一向表现很好。但不知为什么，在时代精神（zeitgeist）以及产品侧的实际使用上，他们似乎总是差一口气。如果说这两个具有里程碑意义的时刻分别是 ChatGPT 和消费级产品，以及 ClaudeCode 和 coding agents，那么至少从使用情况来看，Google 在这两个领域里都没有一个特别有竞争力的产品。并不是说这些产品本身不好，它们其实是不错的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: The models are good, but they haven't really figured out the the latter part, and I'm wondering kinda what you make of that.

paragraphs.zh: 模型是好的，但他们还没有真正搞定后半段，而我有点想知道你怎么看这件事。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: People use Gemini more than we realize.
Right?
You know, it's sort of bundled into Google search at this point, the AI mode thing.

But I I I take the broader point, which is that both of these sort of seminal moments in proving out, you know, the the the consumer experience, which were ChatTPT, as you say, on the consumer side and then more recently with coding.
Neither of those came from Google DeepMind.

paragraphs.zh: 人们使用 Gemini 的程度，可能比我们意识到的更高，对吧？你知道，到这个阶段，它某种程度上已经被打包进 Google Search 里了，就是那个 AI mode 的功能。不过我接受你更大的那个观点：这两个真正奠定了 consumer experience（消费级体验）的关键时刻——就像你说的，一边是消费端的 ChatGPT，另一边则是最近 coding 方向上的进展——这两者都不是来自 Google DeepMind。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And I think what that perhaps shows us is that, you know, partly because of Demis's personality and his intellectual formation, which was PhD in neuroscience, this very broad study of what intelligence might be, a very broad approach therefore to building artificial intelligence.
There's this there's this kind of let's try everything approach to AI research.
Whenever there's like two different paths you could go down, they say, well, we'll do both.
And if we can find the third path, we'll probably do that too.
They're very hedged.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得这也许说明了这样一点：某种程度上，由于 Demis 的个性以及他的 intellectual formation（智识背景），也就是神经科学 PhD，再加上他对 intelligence（智能）可能是什么所做的那种非常宽广的研究，因此他在构建 artificial intelligence（人工智能）时采取的也是一种非常宽泛的路径。这里面有一种“什么都试试看”的 AI research（AI 研究）方法。每当面前有两条不同的路可走时，他们会说，那我们两条都做；如果还能找到第三条路，大概率也会一起做。他们非常对冲。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Right?
Whereas I think Anthropic got to coding because it was willing to take a more concentrated bet.
It never went into the whole field of, you know, generative video, just never had a sort of equivalent.

And, you know, at the same time, OpenAI, being a startup, didn't have the reputational baggage that it had to protect that Google has, and so it willing to put out a chatbot that hallucinated a lot at the beginning.

paragraphs.zh: 对吧？而我认为 Anthropic 之所以能做到 coding，是因为它愿意下一个更集中的赌注。它从来没有进入 generative video（生成式视频）这一整片领域，也从未做过某种对应的尝试。与此同时，OpenAI 作为一家 startup（初创公司），没有 Google 那种必须保护的声誉包袱，所以它愿意推出一个在最开始 hallucinate（产生幻觉、胡编）很多的 chatbot（聊天机器人）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: It honestly has a lot of echoes of of the transformer itself.
Right?
And and, basically, I think OpenAI making the bet of going all in on on scaling transformers, which were obviously invented at Google and Google pursuing a bunch of different paths.
And then to your point, OpenAI starting to do a bunch of things and Anthropic deciding, hey, we're just focusing on coding.
Have you seen kind of a shift in in and DeepMind's focus, I guess, what's played out and given this kind of, it's such a tension, right?

paragraphs.zh: 老实说，这和 transformer 本身很有呼应，不是吗？基本上，我认为 OpenAI 押注的是把 transformer 的 scaling（规模化扩展）一路做到底——而 transformer 显然是由 Google 发明的——与此同时，Google 走的是很多不同的路径。然后就像你说的，OpenAI 开始做很多事情，而 Anthropic 则决定说，嘿，我们就专注于 coding。你是否看到 DeepMind 的重心出现了某种转变？我的意思是，考虑到事情的发展，以及这种张力——这确实是一种很强的张力，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: As a polymath, as someone who's so interested in many facets of AI to focus on a ton of different things versus the thing that's happening at the moment.
Obviously, OpenAI, I think famously has kind of consolidated down and said we were doing too much.
Now we've got to really hone in and catch up to Anthropicana coding models.
How do you think Demis and the DeepMind folks think about this at this

paragraphs.zh: 作为一个 polymath（博学多才的人），作为一个对 AI 的许多侧面都强烈感兴趣的人，到底是把注意力分散到很多不同事情上，还是聚焦于当下正在发生的那个核心机会，这之间始终存在拉扯。显然，我觉得 OpenAI 已经很有代表性地开始收缩战线，并且说过，我们之前做得太多了，现在必须真正聚焦起来，追赶 Anthropicana coding models。你觉得 Demis 和 DeepMind 的人此时此刻会如何看待这个问题？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I think, you know, they do have this tendency to try and do everything.
As I was saying, I don't think that's been cured.
I mean, you know, I was chatting actually just last week with one of the young scientists from that from that shop.
And, you know, that was very much the tenor of what he was telling me.
It's like, you know, we always make multiple bets.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得，你知道，他们确实有一种什么都想做的倾向。就像我刚才说的，我不认为这种情况已经被纠正了。我的意思是，实际上就在上周，我还和那边团队里的一位年轻科学家聊过天。而且，他告诉我的内容在基调上非常符合这一点：就像是，你知道，我们总是会下多个赌注。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: We never really go hard down one avenue.
And and, you know, from a corporate point of view, maybe this makes sense.
I mean, if we if we think about what happened with the consumer side, the sort of general chatbot model, yeah, they were late.
They were late both in trying to develop it after the transformer thing dropped in 2017, and then they were late again in productizing, you know, behind ChatTPT.
But then they caught up by 2025.

paragraphs.zh: 我们其实从来不会在某一条路上真正全力猛冲。而且，你知道，从企业的角度看，也许这有其合理性。我的意思是，如果我们想想 consumer 端发生的事，也就是那种通用 chatbot 模型，是的，他们晚了。他们既是在 2017 年 transformer 那波出来之后才开始尝试开发这类东西，已经晚了一步；之后在把它 productize（产品化）这件事上又晚了一次，落后于 ChatTPT。但到了 2025 年，他们又追上来了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So maybe if you have very deep pockets like Google and a very deep technical bench and tons of human talent and amazing amounts of compute, you know, you can afford you're they're not doing Apple.
Right?
Apple is the absolute extreme where they just say, whatever.
We're not gonna do this AI thing.
We'll just charge people to put it on our iPhone.

paragraphs.zh: 所以也许如果你像 Google 一样资金极其充裕，技术储备也极其深厚，还有大量人才和惊人的 compute（算力），你就承受得起这种做法——他们毕竟不是在学 Apple，对吧？Apple 才是最极端的例子，他们基本就是一句：无所谓。我们不做这个 AI 的事。我们就向别人收费，让它装到我们的 iPhone 上。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's an extremely hands off position towards AI.
Google is much more in it, but it doesn't mind being a couple of years behind because it figures it can catch up.

paragraphs.zh: 这是一种对 AI 极其放手的态度。Google 则深度参与得多，但它并不介意落后个几年，因为它觉得自己可以追上来。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And I wonder at some point whether that that does shift.
Obviously, you've you've, you know, allowed $2,000,000,000,000, plus.
We'll see what they're end up being worth companies to to or or at least in the in the prediction market seem to be worth that, you know, come come up, and and and gain a lot of territory.

And, you know, think I it's it's it'll be really interesting to see going forward, you know, whether that that focused or or that, like, diversity of different things they're pursuing, you know, ends up ends up continuing.
You know, you talk a lot about in the book about, you know, the governance of DeepMind and Google and, like, the way these two organizations work together.

paragraphs.zh: 但我也在想，某个时点这种情况会不会发生变化。显然，你已经让一些估值达到甚至超过 $2,000,000,000,000 的公司——至少在 prediction market（预测市场）里看起来是这个级别——成长起来，并且占据了大量地盘。然后，我觉得，接下来会很有意思的是，看这种专注式路线，或者说这种同时推进很多不同事情的多样化路线，最终会不会持续下去。你在书里也谈了很多 DeepMind 和 Google 的治理问题，比如这两个组织是如何协同运作的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And And I think actually one of the most interesting parts I thought was this DeepMind was considering spinning out of Google.
There was, like, some financing behind that.
They ultimately chose not to.
Why don't you think they ended up, you know, deciding to to spin out of Google?
And I guess in retrospect, was that the right decision?

paragraphs.zh: 而且我觉得，实际上最有意思的部分之一，是 DeepMind 当时曾考虑从 Google 分拆出去。背后似乎还有一些融资支持。最终他们没有这么做。你觉得他们最后为什么没有决定从 Google 分拆？再回头看，那是不是个正确的决定？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
So there was this secret plan called project Mario to spin out.
And Demis was not so happy when I kinda discovered about it from other sources.
I had to speak to the general counsel of Google DeepMind at one point who tried to persuade me I couldn't write about it.
Other people had leaked me the documents and I had them and, you know, definitely had good information.

paragraphs.zh: 是这样的，当时有一个秘密计划，叫 project Mario，就是要分拆出去。Demis 对我从其他渠道发现这件事并不是很高兴。我有一次甚至不得不和 Google DeepMind 的总法律顾问谈，对方试图说服我，说我不能写这件事。可其他人已经把文件泄露给我了，我手上有那些材料，而且，你知道，我掌握的信息显然很扎实。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It was all true, and I wrote it, so whatever.

And the point of this story is is actually partly goes back to your safety thing that that Demis really really wanted to get safety oversight over the Google DeepMind models or just the DeepMind as it was then.
And Google corporate in the mountain view wasn't doing that.
And so he had to have a credible threat of spinning out.
So he went to Reed Hoffman.

paragraphs.zh: 这件事完全是真的，所以我还是写了，反正就是这样。而这个故事的重点，其实部分还是回到你前面说的 safety（安全）问题：Demis 真的非常希望对 Google DeepMind 的模型——或者说当时还是 DeepMind 本身——建立 safety oversight（安全监督）。但 Google 在 Mountain View 的公司层面并没有这么做。所以他必须拿出一个可信的“分拆威胁”。于是他去找了 Reed Hoffman。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Reid Hoffman pledged a billion dollars to finance a spin out and Demis used that to kind of pressure Google.
Although I don't think he ever mentioned that Reid Hoffman had done that but he he he pushed he pushed Google knowing that he had this back pocket option.
And I spoke to a lot of the advisers who were in the room when DeepMind was figuring out, you know, whether to go with a Reid Hoffman spin out option.

And there were those who said, look, you should spin out because then you'll be an independent startup and you'll have all the incentives and the kind of, you know, alertness and the and the and the flexibility, the agility that go with that.
You can, you know, give all your team, you know, very high powered financial incentives linked to your performance, and it'll be awesome and you should spin out.

paragraphs.zh: Reid Hoffman 承诺出资 10 亿美元，为分拆融资，而 Demis 就用这一点来某种程度上向 Google 施压。虽然我觉得他可能从未明确提过 Reid Hoffman 做了这件事，但他确实是在知道自己手里有这个备用选项的前提下，去推动、去施压 Google。我还采访了很多当时在场的顾问——他们就在 DeepMind 认真权衡是否要采用 Reid Hoffman 分拆方案的时候在那个房间里。其中有些人说，听着，你们应该分拆，因为那样你们就会成为一家独立 startup（初创公司），并拥有与之相伴的所有激励机制，以及那种警觉性、灵活性，还有敏捷性。你们还可以给整个团队提供非常强力、并且与业绩挂钩的财务激励，那会非常棒，所以你们应该分拆。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And then Demes' view in the end was, you know, it's it's legally, it's gonna be a there may be a court battle over it, you know, whether we have the right to do that.
And I just wanna do science.
I wanna not be distracted by legal fights.
I want access to tons of compute.
I'm staying in.

paragraphs.zh: 然后 Demis 最后的看法是，你知道，从法律上说，这件事很可能会因此打官司，也就是我们是否有权这么做。我只是想做科学。我不想被法律纠纷分散精力。我想获得海量 compute（算力）。我要留下来。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And so that was the call he made.
Was it right?
Was it wrong?
Well, I mean, did lead to him getting a Nobel Prize, right, in 2020.
You know, it's a small matter of a Nobel Prize.

paragraphs.zh: 所以这就是他当时做出的决定。这是对是错？嗯，我是说，这确实让他拿到了 Nobel Prize，对吧，还是在 2020 年。你知道，只不过是个“小事”——Nobel Prize。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Then, you know, in in 2020, so a year after he gave up that fight with his parent company, he ships AlphaFold, the protein folding prediction model, and that gets him the Nobel Prize.

paragraphs.zh: 然后，你知道，到了 2020 年，也就是他放弃和母公司那场斗争一年之后，他推出了 AlphaFold，这个蛋白质折叠预测模型，而这也让他拿到了 Nobel Prize。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Kind of way you just framed it was more of like, hey.
This was part of negotiating leverage to to, you know, get some of the safety stuff.
I mean, how seriously do you think the DeepMind team was considering this at the time?

paragraphs.zh: 你刚才的表述方式有点像是在说，嘿，这更多是一种谈判筹码，用来，怎么说呢，争取到一些 safety（安全）方面的安排。我是说，你觉得 DeepMind 团队当时到底有多认真地在考虑这件事？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Well, I think they always, you know, didn't quite they never quite got to that decision point in their own heads.
Like, wanted the plan b spin out option because why not have that?
And they weren't sure how to then use it in the negotiation with Google.
And I think they determined never to tell Google explicitly that they had that, but a kind of hint that, you know, there might be something in the background.
And if you don't do what we want with safety oversight, we may do something you don't you don't expect kind of thing.

paragraphs.zh: 嗯，我觉得他们一直都没有真正在自己脑子里走到那个做决定的节点。就像，他们想保留一个 plan b，也就是 spin out（拆分出去）这个选项，毕竟为什么不留着呢？但他们也不确定该怎么在和 Google 的谈判中使用它。我觉得他们最后决定，绝不明确告诉 Google 他们有这个选项，而只是稍微暗示一下，你知道，背后可能还有点别的东西。如果你们不按我们希望的方式来做 safety oversight（安全监督），那我们可能会做出一些你们意想不到的事，大概就是这种意思。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But it you know, ultimately, they never really waived that threat explicitly and they never exercised a threat even though Google didn't give them the safety oversight that they wanted.
So in the end, I mean, it's in some sense, it's a story of the naivete of the of the founders, both Demis himself and Mustafa Soleiman, his co founder who's now at Microsoft.
I mean, they went into this, you know, not quite sure what their end game was.
And in the end, they never really used that spin out op I mean, they they not no. In the end, they didn't use the spin out option.

paragraphs.zh: 但是，你知道，归根结底，他们从来没有真正明确亮出过这个威胁；而且即便 Google 没有给他们想要的 safety oversight（安全监督），他们也从未真的把这个威胁付诸实施。所以到头来，我是说，从某种意义上讲，这体现的是创始人的天真，既包括 Demis 本人，也包括他的联合创始人 Mustafa Soleiman——他现在在 Microsoft。我的意思是，他们当初进入这件事时，并不完全清楚自己的最终盘算是什么。而到最后，他们也始终没有真正使用那个 spin out 选项——我是说，他们没——对。最终，他们没有使用 spin out 这个选项。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And so sometimes spending three years going back and forth to Mountain View with this was a waste of time.

paragraphs.zh: 所以有时候，围绕这件事和 Mountain View 来来回回折腾三年，算是浪费时间。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I'm wondering in in the course of the conversations that you that you had with Demis, I mean, obviously, we talked about that AI safety summit already and kinda realizing, yeah, that was not ideal.
Are there any other things that he regrets, I guess, about how the whole past decade has unfolded?

paragraphs.zh: 我在想，在你和 Demis 的这些谈话过程中，显然，我们刚才已经谈到那个 AI safety summit（AI 安全峰会）了，也有点意识到，嗯，那次处理得并不理想。那除此之外，关于过去这十年整个事情的发展方式，他还有没有什么别的后悔之处？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: What does he regret?
I'd say that he would certainly not regret, spending all that energy on AI for science.

paragraphs.zh: 他后悔什么吗？我会说，他当然不会后悔把那么多精力投入到 AI for science 上。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I love that anecdote in your book, by the way, that, like, the day they won AlphaGo, AlphaGo, yeah, won the the the you know, beat the best player in the world.
He was already on to Bio, and someone picked it up on a mic, which I which I just thought was, talking about an ambitious, and always moving, leader.

paragraphs.zh: 顺便说一句，我很喜欢你书里的那个轶事：就在他们赢下 AlphaGo 的那天，AlphaGo，对，赢了——你知道的——击败了世界上最强的棋手，而他已经转去关注 Bio 了，后来有人从麦克风里录到了这件事。我当时就觉得，这真是一个雄心勃勃、而且永远不停向前的 leader（领导者）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Right.
Right.
He rests on his laurels for about ten seconds, and then he says the next thing we're gonna do is we're gonna solve protein folding.
Yeah.
That that was amazing.

paragraphs.zh: 对，对。他大概就沉浸在胜利里十秒钟，然后就说，我们接下来要做的事，就是解决 protein folding（蛋白质折叠）。对，那真是太惊人了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But I think, you know, the the serious point here is that he not only got a Nobel Prize out of it, he also views this, I think, correctly as absolutely central to the whole acceptability of artificial intelligence in society writ large.
If AI doesn't deliver, you know, clear benefits for humans, and it's just like lots of job disruption, which may be good for productivity, but it's kinda painful for people on the receiving end.
I'm not sure that politically AI will really be rolled out with some massive without some huge backlash.
So so I think, you know, both from it's good to advance science.
You know, I like winning a Nobel Prize, but also AI can't succeed without this kind of science stuff.

paragraphs.zh: 但我觉得，这里严肃的一点是，他不仅因此拿到了 Nobel Prize，他也——而且我认为这是正确的——把这件事看作是 artificial intelligence 在整个社会层面被接受的绝对核心。如果 AI 不能带来对人类明确的好处，而只是造成大量 job disruption（就业冲击），这也许对 productivity（生产率）有好处，但对承受其后果的人来说会相当痛苦。那么从政治上讲，我不确定 AI 是否真能大规模 rollout（推广），而不引发巨大的 backlash（反弹）。所以我认为，一方面，推进 science（科学）本身就是好事。你知道，拿个 Nobel Prize 也不错；但另一方面，如果没有这种科学方面的成果，AI 也不可能成功。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I think he doesn't regret that for a second.
You know, does he regret not being faster onto chatbots?
Yeah.
Of course.

I mean, it would have been better to understand, you know, like Ilya Satsuka did at OpenAI, you know, the moment the transformer dropped, Ilya is like jumping out of his chair running down the corridor going to find Alec Radford saying, hey, we're gonna build a language model based on this transformer architecture.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得他对此一秒钟都不后悔。你知道，他会不会后悔自己在 chatbots（聊天机器人）这件事上跟进得不够快？会，当然会。我的意思是，如果当初能像 OpenAI 的 Ilya Satsuka 那样更早看明白，那肯定会更好——你知道，就在 transformer 刚出来的那一刻，Ilya 一下子从椅子上跳起来，冲出走廊去找 Alec Radford，说，嘿，我们要基于这个 transformer architecture（Transformer 架构）来构建一个 language model（语言模型）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I mean, on the day the paper dropped because he had this prepared mind, you know, since his PhD, he had been thinking about how to deal with, you know, sequential data like text.
And so when the Transformer Biblical came out, he immediately saw the significance.
And of course, it would have been great for DeepMind if they had had that same perception that quickly and it took them instead, you know, two, three years to get there.
And Demis, you know, frankly still has a bit of a blind spot about this.
You know, when I say to him, well, you were sort of three years late.

paragraphs.zh: 我的意思是，就在那篇 paper 刚发布的当天，因为他早就做好了思想准备。你知道，从 PhD 起，他就一直在思考如何处理像 text（文本）这样的 sequential data（序列数据）。所以当 Transformer 这部“圣经”般的重要成果一出来时，他立刻就看到了其中的意义。当然，如果 DeepMind 当时也能这么快意识到这一点，那就太好了；但事实上，他们花了两三年才赶上来。而且说实话，Demis 到现在在这件事上仍然有一点 blind spot（认知盲点）。你知道，当我对他说，嗯，你们其实晚了大概三年。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: He goes, no. No. No. No. We had Chinchilla.

paragraphs.zh: 他就会说，不，不，不，不。我们有 Chinchilla。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: We had these other models.
And I'm saying, yeah.
Yeah.
But you didn't release those till the end of twenty twenty.
And Ilya by then had been working on it for three and a half years, so you were late.

paragraphs.zh: 我们还有其他这些 models（模型）。而我会说，对，对，但是你们直到 2020 年底才发布那些东西。而到那时，Ilya 已经在这件事上做了三年半了，所以你们就是晚了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But he he really resists that, and I think that is because he he does regret that.

paragraphs.zh: 但他确实非常抗拒这一点，我觉得那是因为他对此确实感到后悔。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Well, I thought it was interesting too, you know, the way you kind of put put it in your book is that, you know, part of the resistance maybe the transformer architecture was this.

He obviously comes from such a deep neuroscience background and this belief that the path to artificial general intelligence has to flow through something that looks much more like the way humans learn and the human brain, which obviously was the RL world and less of just you know, how how can you really understand what it is to be a human by consuming the Internet?
I think your point was actually it turns out you can understand it the the way more than one might have anticipated from scaling these these models.

paragraphs.zh: 嗯，我也觉得这很有意思。你在书里的表述大概是这样：这种抗拒的一部分原因，也许就在于 transformer architecture（Transformer 架构）本身。他显然有非常深厚的 neuroscience（神经科学）背景，并且相信，通往 artificial general intelligence（AGI，通用人工智能）的路径，必须经过某种更像人类学习方式、更像人脑的东西；而那显然更属于 RL（reinforcement learning，强化学习）的世界，而不是那种“只靠吞下整个 Internet，就真的能理解什么是人类吗”的思路。我觉得你的观点其实是，事实证明，通过对这些模型进行 scaling（规模扩展），它们对这一点的理解程度，远远超出了人们原本的预期。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.

And by the way, I think there may be some version of that same dilemma with robotics In order to train robotics and simulation, you need these real world simulations and probably video is one important tool for building those simulations.
So potentially, if DeepMind gets the right bet on the right pathway to much better robotics, it has the it has what it takes.
But we'll see whether, you know, there's some version of what what went wrong with language and the transformer model is that just underestimated the importance of language to super intelligence.

And then maybe, you know, to get robotics right, Is it, for example, something that you're gonna do by building physical robots and having them crash and break their arms, you know, in some kind of physical lab?

paragraphs.zh: 对。顺便说一句，我觉得在 robotics（机器人）领域，可能也存在某种类似的困境。为了在 simulation（仿真）中训练机器人，你需要这些现实世界的模拟，而 video（视频）很可能是构建这些模拟的重要工具之一。所以，如果 DeepMind 在通往更强 robotics 的正确路径上押对了注，它确实具备所需的条件。但我们还得看，language（语言）和 transformer model（Transformer 模型）上曾经出过的问题，是否会以某种形式重演——也就是，人们只是低估了 language 对 superintelligence（超级智能）的重要性。那么，也许要把 robotics 做对，举例来说，真的是要靠制造实体机器人，让它们在某种 physical lab（实体实验室）里不断碰撞、摔坏机械臂来实现吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: But it's an interesting, you know, point because actually, you know, too many perils in the robotics space where there's, you know, ten, fifteen different approaches that people are trying.
And I imagine if you're Google and, like, given the way DeepMind's approach other things, you'll kinda put your hand in a bunch of these approaches.
And meanwhile, you'll have startups that were like, we're all in on, you know, x approach or y approach, and it'll be interesting to see how that plays out.
Because you can certainly empathize today when the recipe is not clear.

It it's it's not particularly irrational for for DeepMind to keep their options open, but also leaves you vulnerable to someone that that really goes all in on the transformer or encode or whatever the next, you know, equivalent is in the robotics space.

paragraphs.zh: 不过这是个很有意思的点，因为实际上，robotics 领域里的变量和风险太多了，现在大家大概在尝试十种、十五种不同的方法。我可以想象，如果你是 Google，考虑到 DeepMind 处理其他事情的一贯方式，你大概会同时把手伸进好几种路线里。与此同时，也会有 startups（初创公司）说，我们就全押在 x 路线或者 y 路线上。接下来会怎么发展，会很有意思。因为在今天这种“配方”还不清楚的时候，这其实是可以理解的——DeepMind 保持多种选择并不特别不理性；但这也会让你更容易受到那种真正全力押注 transformer、encoder（编码器），或者 robotics 领域下一个同等级关键范式的人的冲击。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And I and I I think here actually there's a larger fascinating question about whether venture backed innovation beats sort of hyperscaler, you know, tech behemoth, you know, AI approaches.
Because

paragraphs.zh: 完全同意。而且我觉得，这里其实还有一个更大、也很吸引人的问题：venture-backed（风投支持的）创新，是否会胜过某种 hyperscaler（超大规模云厂商）式的、tech behemoth（科技巨头）式的 AI 路线。因为——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I have a biased opinion on that one.

paragraphs.zh: 对这个问题，我是有立场偏向的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, that that's fair.
But but I think but but, you know, I'm less biased.
But but, you know, I've written about a hyperscaler now, but my previous book was about venture.

paragraphs.zh: 对，对，这很公平。不过我觉得——你知道——我的偏见没那么强。虽然我现在写的这本书讲的是一家 hyperscaler，但我上一本书写的是 venture。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I kind of done both.
But I think it's kind of a interestingly balanced thing because, know, in one sense, this is a very capital intensive project.
You need very deep pockets.
If you look at OpenAI's ability to fight Google over the long haul on generative AI, You know, I wrote in the New York Times in January that I thought OpenAI had a 50% chance of going bust by next It

paragraphs.zh: 我算是两边都接触过。但我觉得这件事有一种很有意思的平衡性，因为从一个角度看，这是一项非常 capital intensive（资本密集型）的事业，你需要非常雄厚的财力。就拿 OpenAI 在 generative AI（生成式 AI）上长期与 Google 竞争的能力来说吧，我在一月份写给 New York Times 的文章里曾说过，我认为 OpenAI 到明年破产的概率有 50%。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: sounds like it's still 50, by the way?

paragraphs.zh: 顺便问一下，听起来你还是觉得是五五开，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
I mean, you know, I said fail.
I mean, I I I what I mean is it it sells itself as a disc at a discount to to some hyperscaler.
And I think, you know, I've I've been impressed by some of the, you know, expenditure cuts they've done.
You know, canceling Sora was a smart move.

paragraphs.zh: 对。我是说，你知道，我说的是失败。我的意思是，它最终会把自己以折价的方式卖给某个 hyperscaler（超大规模云服务商）。而且我觉得，你知道，他们做的一些开支削减让我印象挺深的。你知道，取消 Sora 是个明智之举。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: They've done some impressive cost cutting, so I give them credit for that.
On the other hand, of course, the sort of rumors around Sam being replaced by Brett Taylor are pretty widespread, and it's just a general sense of, you know, the leadership is damaged goods.
Yeah.
I think on net, I'm still around fifty fifty that they fail next by kinda next summer.
And and that's not because they don't have great tech because they do.

paragraphs.zh: 他们确实做了一些令人印象深刻的成本削减，这点我得给他们肯定。另一方面，当然，关于 Sam 会被 Brett Taylor 取代的传闻也相当普遍，整体给人的感觉就是，领导层已经成了“有瑕疵的资产”。对，所以综合来看，我还是大概维持五五开的判断：他们会在接下来、差不多到明年夏天之前失败。之所以这么说，并不是因为他们没有很棒的技术——因为他们确实有。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I mean, the tech is great.
It's just the business model.
It's the problematic.
And it's problematic because you're up against Google, which just has unlimited amounts of cash to spend you into the ground.
And so in one sense, this looks like a platform shift that benefits the incumbents.

paragraphs.zh: 我的意思是，技术很棒。问题只是在商业模式上。而且这个商业模式之所以有问题，是因为你的对手是 Google，它有几乎无限的现金，足以一路把你压垮。所以从某种意义上说，这看起来像是一场有利于 incumbents（既有巨头）的 platform shift（平台迁移）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: On the other hand, as we've been discussing, when you have the luxury of unlimited amounts of talent and compute and money, you tend not to make these strategic concentrated bets.
And if you're up against a bunch of different venture backed startups that each of them makes different, you know, highly concentrated bets, then maybe one of those bets work out.

paragraphs.zh: 但另一方面，正如我们刚才讨论的那样，当你同时拥有几乎无限的人才、compute（算力）和资金时，你往往反而不会去做这种战略上高度集中的下注。而如果你的对手是一群由 venture（风投）支持的 startup（初创公司），它们每一家都在做不同的、高度集中的押注，那么也许其中某一个押注就会成功。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: But it is a game of very thin margins.
Right?

I mean, if if you take Anthropic, I think there was always a question about whether they would be able to raise enough capital to stay in this race or or or basically, you know, have to sell or or be deeply embedded with a hyperscaler, which they are to some extent, and it felt like it might be headed on that track with with either Google or Amazon.
And then, you know, right at the moment where you might have wondered, hey.
Can they keep raising the amount of capital that's required to stay in this game?

paragraphs.zh: 但这终究是个利润极薄的游戏，对吧？我的意思是，如果拿 Anthropic 来说，我觉得一直都有一个问题，那就是他们是否能融到足够多的资金，继续留在这场竞赛里；或者说，他们最终是否基本上不得不出售，或者与某个 hyperscaler（超大规模云服务商）深度绑定——他们在某种程度上其实已经是这样了——而且当时感觉他们可能正朝着那条路走，要么靠 Google，要么靠 Amazon。然后，就在那个时刻，你可能会开始怀疑：他们还能不能继续融到留在这场游戏中所必需的那种规模的资本？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: The coding models hit, and they hit in a in a huge way.
And and now, you know, Anthropic is is the the hottest company in the in the bay.
But, like, it's a really interesting counterfactual if that stuff had hit six months later, twelve months later.
It certainly wasn't inevitable, that that it was really gonna start working then.
And, yeah, a lot of this stuff I mean, it seems you can write a very clean narrative in in retrospect, but it's not, an inevitability that things play out that way.

paragraphs.zh: 结果 coding models（代码模型）爆发了，而且是以一种非常巨大的方式爆发。于是现在，你知道，Anthropic 成了整个湾区最火的公司。但如果那些东西晚六个月、十二个月才爆发，那会是个非常有意思的反事实情境。当然，这件事并不是注定会在那个时候开始真正奏效的。对，这里面很多事情都是这样——事后看，你似乎可以写出一个非常干净、清晰的叙事，但事情并不是必然会那样发展。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I wanna go back to something you said on on Demis, which was him obviously focusing on science and this kind of belief that, hey, we have to show that AI can have beneficial outcomes for society or or else the world will turn against it.
And one thing I'm struck by in particular, you you kind of alluded to it right at the beginning that you couldn't put Demis on the cover because people wouldn't recognize, you know, who he was.

And in in many ways, like, the people that the world associates with AI today are are Sam and Dario, They're kind of taking a lot of the oxygen in the public conversation about how people think about AI.
I'm wondering, one, how Demes feels about that, and two, over time, do you think he feels like or will have to become more of a public figure than he is today to shape that conversation?
Obviously, he's deeply passionate about this stuff in a way that's more analogous with how he feels.

paragraphs.zh: 我想回到你刚才关于 Demis 说的一点，也就是他显然更专注于科学，以及那种信念：我们必须证明 AI 能为社会带来有益的结果，否则整个世界都会转而反对它。有一点尤其让我很在意，你在一开始其实也隐约提到过——你没法把 Demis 放上封面，因为人们认不出他是谁。而在很多方面，今天世界提到 AI 时联想到的人，其实是 Sam 和 Dario；他们在关于人们如何理解 AI 的公共讨论中，某种程度上占据了大量注意力。我想知道，第一，Demis 对此是什么感受；第二，从长远来看，你觉得他是否会认为，或者是否不得不比今天更加成为一个公众人物，才能去塑造这场讨论？显然，他对这些议题怀有非常深的热情，而且那种投入方式也和他一贯的感受更为一致。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
First of all, to be clear, the compromise my publisher, struck was to have him on the cover, but to kind of fuzz it up so that he looks like a kind of enigmatic geek.
And if you don't recognize him, it's still a cool picture.
So that's Fair enough.
What they did.

paragraphs.zh: 是的。首先，先说清楚，我的出版方最后达成的折中方案是把他放到封面上，但稍微做得模糊一点，让他看起来像一个有点神秘的 geek（技术宅）。这样一来，如果你认不出他，那它依然是一张很酷的照片。所以他们这么做，我觉得也算合理。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But, yeah, I think to your question, you know, Dennis does understand that he needs to be out there more.

He's good at kind of self narrativizing in one way, which is that, you know, he's the kind of retrospective story of his journey up until where he is now is something that he's been good at communicating.
He spends more than thirty hours talking to me and of course of course there is a reason for that.
But but also, you know, there was the documentary, The Thinking Game, which a lot of people have seen.

And before that, there was a documentary about AlphaGo which didn't get made by mistake, you know, that they actually brought a documentary team with them to Seoul when they played that game against the Seoul Go champion.

paragraphs.zh: 不过，关于你的问题，我确实觉得 Dennis 明白自己需要更多地出现在公众视野里。在某种意义上，他很擅长 self-narrativizing（自我叙事）：也就是说，他很会讲述自己一路走到今天的那种回顾性故事。他和我谈了三十多个小时，当然，当然这里面是有原因的。不过另外，你知道，也有那部很多人看过的纪录片 The Thinking Game。在那之前，还有一部关于 AlphaGo 的纪录片，而那也不是偶然拍出来的；当年他们去 Seoul 与 Seoul 围棋冠军下那场比赛时，实际上是专门带着一个纪录片团队一起去的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So so there is a there is this retrospective storytelling which he's good at.
What he doesn't do is prospective.
He doesn't like Sam, you know, has or at least in his heyday had the ability to kill the attention on a new DeepMind release simply by putting out a couple of tweets.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，他确实很擅长这种回顾性的讲故事方式。但他不做的是前瞻性的叙事。他不像 Sam 那样，你知道的，拥有——或者至少在他的巅峰时期拥有——这样一种能力：只要发几条 tweets，就能直接抢走一次新的 DeepMind 发布的全部注意力。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: They would always go one day before.
Like, they they know something was gonna happen.
And

paragraphs.zh: 他们总是会提前一天动作。就好像他们知道有什么事要发生一样。然后——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
And when we say go one day before, we mean, you know, just trail something on on on x.
Just put out a couple of posts and and and, you know, not even release something.

But that was but because Sam's following on x is, like, five, six times Demis's, the echo chamber is so much stronger that he could really dominate the narrative even when the other guys were releasing the product.
And that's a problem both in terms of product adoption for DeepMind, but also like talent recruitment.

paragraphs.zh: 对。这里我们说“提前一天”，意思就是，先在 x 上预告一下。发上几条帖子，甚至都不用真的发布什么东西。但问题是，因为 Sam 在 x 上的追随者规模大概是 Demis 的五到六倍，那个回音室效应就强得多，所以哪怕实际发布产品的是另一方，他也真的可以主导整个叙事。这对 DeepMind 来说是个问题，不仅关系到产品采用（product adoption），也关系到人才招聘（talent recruitment）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You know, controlling the narrative does matter.
And I think they kind of get that over at Google DeepMind.
Whether they quite have the ability to fight it, we'll see.
I mean what, you know, what they don't do is is do what, you know, Darius has which is pick a hut, you know, public fight with the Pentagon.
Know, unreleased mythos.

paragraphs.zh: 你知道，控制叙事确实很重要。我觉得 Google DeepMind 那边某种程度上也明白这一点。至于他们是否真的有能力去对抗这种局面，我们还得看。我的意思是，他们不会做的事情，是像 Darius 那样，主动挑起一场——比如说——和 Pentagon 的公开冲突。你知道，再加上那种尚未发布的 mythos（神话式光环）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: All these things which just, you know, immediately turn you into a massive celebrity.
And Demis, in some ways, is too sensible to pick a public fight with the government.
And so maybe that makes him less notorious and, you know, maybe it's it's a kind of byproduct of strategic caution that he's a bit less front of mind in the media.

paragraphs.zh: 所有这些事情都会让你立刻变成一个超级名人。而 Demis 在某些方面又太理性了，不会去主动和政府打公开战。所以，也许这让他没那么“臭名昭著”，而且，你知道，也许他在媒体中没那么占据焦点，本身就是一种战略谨慎的副产品。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I'm curious like what you ended up learning about the extent to which that does or doesn't attract researchers.
Right?
Obviously, a huge part of the battle here is the battle for talent.
You've had I mean, Google has incredible researchers.
You've had folks like, you know, like Jack Ray, who you talk about in the in the book who left, then came back, then left again.

paragraphs.zh: 我很好奇，你最后了解到的是，这种事在多大程度上会——或者不会——吸引研究人员，对吧？显然，这里战斗中一个非常大的部分，就是人才争夺战。我的意思是，Google 拥有极其出色的研究人员。也有像 Jack Ray 这样的人——你在书里提到过他——他离开了，后来又回来，然后又离开了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: You know, how do you think about or or in in talking with folks, the types of people that are attracted to DeepMind, the extent to which they, you know, do or don't need to change their hiring brand to kinda stay on on par with the others?

paragraphs.zh: 你觉得，在和人交流时，谈到那些被 DeepMind 吸引的人群类型，你会怎么看：为了和其他竞争者保持同一水平，他们在多大程度上需要、或者不需要，去调整自己的招聘品牌？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
It's a great question.
I think it sort of overlaps with what we were saying about the rivalry between the, you know, deep pocketed hyperscaler and the and the venture model.
Because I think, you know, for Jack Ray, when he left DeepMind and went to OpenAI, he explained to me that, you know, this was because there was a concentrated bet on language models at OpenAI.
And that's all they cared about at the time.

paragraphs.zh: 对，这是个很好的问题。我觉得这在某种程度上和我们刚才谈到的那种竞争有关：一边是资金雄厚的 hyperscaler（超大规模云厂商）模式，另一边是 venture（风投）模式。因为我想，Jack Ray 当年离开 DeepMind 去 OpenAI 时，他对我解释说，这是因为 OpenAI 当时把赌注高度集中押在 language models（语言模型）上。而且那时候他们在意的就只有这个。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And that's what Jack was doing.
And so he wanted to be there.
And I think, you know, that would be true today in robotics.
There'll be certain researchers who are really great and they wanna go somewhere where there's one concentrated bet which they believe in themselves.
And then they will just, you know, work all out to to be part of that team and and to to realize what they think is the right path.

paragraphs.zh: 而 Jack 做的正是这个方向。所以他想去那里。我觉得，今天在 robotics（机器人）领域也会是这样。会有一些非常优秀的研究人员，他们想去一个地方，那里只有一个他们自己也真正相信的集中押注。然后他们就会，你知道，全力以赴地成为那个团队的一部分，并实现他们认为正确的路径。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Whereas, if you put a robotics researcher in this big, careful, diversified, you know, lab and say, you know, we really believe in you, but we also believe in these other five bets we're making at the same time.
You don't feel so good about it.
You know, there's a kind of passion piece that's that's missing.

paragraphs.zh: 相比之下，如果你把一个 robotics researcher（机器人研究员）放进这样一个庞大、谨慎、而且多元分散的 lab（实验室）里，然后对他说，我们真的很看好你，但我们也同样相信另外五个同时在推进的押注，那感觉就没那么好了。你知道，这里面会少了一种激情层面的东西。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Well, what do you make of the decision you know, obviously, the AlphaFold work eventually got folded out or or spun out into, you know, isomorphic, right, in a different company that kind of I mean, Alphabet still owns a bunch, but is a separate effort.
Like, does that make sense, or is that a a a path forward you think will be repeated?

paragraphs.zh: 那你怎么看这个决定：显然，AlphaFold 的工作最后被拆分出来，或者说 spun out（分拆）成了 isomorphic，对吧，放进了一家不同的公司里。虽然 Alphabet 仍然持有很大一部分股份，但它已经是一个独立推进的项目了。像这样做合理吗？还是说你觉得这会成为一种会被反复采用的前进路径？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's a great question.
I mean, I I think, isomorphic does have that effect.
I I think the spinout was also about trying to be able to do partnerships with pharma big pharma part you know, partners.
Maybe that was a bit easier in a in a spunout format.

paragraphs.zh: 这是个很好的问题。我是说，我觉得 isomorphic 的确会带来那种效果。我也认为，这次 spinout（分拆）还有一个目的，就是为了能够和 pharma（制药行业）的大型 pharma partners（制药合作伙伴）开展合作。也许用分拆出去的组织形式来做，这会稍微更容易一些。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Just because they wouldn't wanna work with Google directly or something?
Or?

paragraphs.zh: 只是因为他们不想直接和 Google 合作之类的吗？还是？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Maybe yeah.
And maybe maybe also just the sense was that this by itself as a freestanding thing could become so big.
And there'd been this history of Google and DeepMind separately doing AI for health.
And health has got its own politics, its own kind of, you know, very long lead times.
You know, it it they they felt it just needed to be in a different home.

paragraphs.zh: 也许吧。也许还有一种想法是，这件事本身如果作为一个独立存在的项目，规模可能会变得非常大。而且过去 Google 和 DeepMind 也一直各自有做 AI for health（医疗 AI）的历史。health（医疗健康）这个领域有它自己的政治性，也有它那种非常长的 lead times（前置周期）。他们可能觉得，这件事就是需要放在一个不同的归属之下。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So they did that.
But I I think you're raising a good point that there might have been an extra reason to do it which is that you you give people the option of working somewhere where they are the bet.
Their thing is the thing.
And I think that's a super important recruiting tool.
I also would note, I mean, it's probably, you know fairly well known in the valley I guess.

paragraphs.zh: 所以他们确实那样做了。但我觉得你提出了一个很好的观点：这么做也许还有一个额外原因，就是你给了人们一个选择，让他们可以去一个“自己才是押注本身”的地方工作。也就是说，他们做的那件事就是最核心的那件事。我认为这是一个超级重要的招聘工具。还有，我想说，这在 valley 里大概也算是，怎么说，比较广为人知吧。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But you know, Anthropics churn is very low relative to everybody else.

And I think that shows you that if you if you're kind of identity as a leader is like Dario where you're kind of out there and unfiltered about your extreme concern for safety and your extreme concern for responsibility and social impact.
You write these long essays.
You know, it it probably means that some people would never join you because they think you're a bit wacky.
But the ones who do join absolutely love you and believe in you, and there's this kind of amazing loyalty, which is kind of special.

paragraphs.zh: 不过你知道，Anthropic 的 churn（人员流失率）相对于其他所有公司都很低。我觉得这说明，如果你作为领导者的 identity（身份认同/风格）有点像 Dario，那种非常公开、毫不修饰地表达自己对 safety（安全）、responsibility（责任）和 social impact（社会影响）的强烈担忧的人，而且还会写这些长篇文章——那很可能意味着有些人永远不会加入你，因为他们觉得你有点古怪。但那些真的加入的人，会非常喜欢你、非常相信你，于是就形成了一种很惊人的忠诚度，这一点很特别。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I mean, another kind of key character in in in your work that we haven't talked about is, you know, David Silver, who obviously played a key role in in a bunch of the reinforcement learning work and was was one of the early, collaborators with Demis on a bunch of things.
You know, he he obviously think, you know, since publishing your book, he recently left, DeepMind, right, to start another company.
I'm curious what you made of that.

paragraphs.zh: 我的意思是，在你的叙述里，还有另一个我们还没谈到的关键人物，就是 David Silver。他显然在一系列 reinforcement learning（强化学习）工作中发挥了关键作用，而且也是早期和 Demis 在很多项目上的合作者之一。你知道，自从你的书出版之后，他最近离开了 DeepMind，对吧，去创办另一家公司了。我很好奇你怎么看这件事。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I think it kind of fits your narrative here, actually.

I mean, in the sense that he was this he he is this intensely determined believer in reinforcement learning to a point where I think most of his colleagues at DeepMind ended up thinking it was just too much.
You know, he was the hero when, first of all, the Atari, you know, game playing system that DeepMind rolled out 2012, 2013, you know, made these incredible breakthroughs.
And, you know, that was almost like ImageNet but for early agents.
And David Silver was a key person in that.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得这其实很符合你这里的整体叙事。因为某种意义上说，他过去是、现在也是那种对 reinforcement learning（强化学习）抱有极强信念的人，坚定到我觉得 DeepMind 的大多数同事最后都认为这有点过头了。你知道，他曾经是那个时代的英雄之一：首先是 DeepMind 在 2012、2013 年推出的 Atari 游戏系统取得了这些令人难以置信的突破。那几乎就像是早期 agent（智能体）领域的 ImageNet，而 David Silver 是其中的关键人物。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And then there was AlphaGo and then there was AlphaZero, which I was, you know, pretty much all learning from reinforcement learning and no I mean, there was deep learning, but it was but it was it was heavily skewed towards the reinforcement learning side.
And and then came this paper from David Silver and Rich Sutton, his PhD supervisor called, what was it called, Experience is Enough I think it was something like that.
And basically learn from experience, learn from reinforcement learning, don't learn from data was the message of that paper.
And David is just very very hard over on that vision that, you know, learning from data is inferior because the data includes mistakes.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 接着有 AlphaGo，然后是 AlphaZero；在我看来，这些几乎都主要是在从 reinforcement learning（强化学习）中学习，当然也有 deep learning（深度学习），但整体上明显更偏向 reinforcement learning 这一边。然后 David Silver 和他的 PhD supervisor（博士导师）Rich Sutton 发了一篇论文，叫——叫什么来着——我想应该是 Experience is Enough，差不多是这个名字。那篇论文传达的信息基本上就是：要从经验中学习、从 reinforcement learning 中学习，而不是从 data（数据）中学习。David 对这个愿景是非常非常坚定的；在他看来，从 data 中学习是更低一等的，因为 data 里包含错误，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: They're kind of if you train if you take the Go analogy, if you train on a human Go player's past games, even if it's expert games, those players don't have perfect understanding of Go.
And you need to get beyond that to have super intelligence.
And so you need to learn the machine needs to learn from its own experience, not rely on the kinda crystallized knowledge of humans passed on through text or other kinds of data.
He is such a believer in that that it's all agents for him, only agents, and they have to learn from themselves.

And I think, you know, Demis told me once or actually more than once that, you know, that kind of approach may ultimately win in some future when you already have AGI and now you're just sort of like perfecting it into even greater superintelligence.

paragraphs.zh: 大致是这样：如果用 Go 来类比，如果你拿人类棋手过去的对局来训练——哪怕是高手对局——这些棋手对 Go 的理解也不是完美的。而如果你想达到 superintelligence（超级智能），你就必须超越这一点。所以机器需要从自己的 experience（经验）中学习，而不是依赖人类通过 text（文本）或其他形式 data 传下来的那种已经结晶化的知识。他对这一点的信念强到，对他来说就是全都是 agent，只有 agent，而且它们必须从自己那里学习。我想，Demis 曾经跟我说过一次——其实不止一次——这种路径最终也许会在未来胜出：当你已经拥有了 AGI，然后你要做的只是把它继续打磨成更高程度的 superintelligence。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Because ultimately, yes, you know, if you if the machine learns from its own data, that is purer and ultimately will get you further.
But to try and get you to that AGI, you need to bootstrap yourself with existing data, and that's what the whole language model revolution showed us.
That for a long time, there wasn't much there was almost no reinforcement learning unless you count reinforcement learning from human

paragraphs.zh: 因为归根结底，没错，如果机器从自己的 data 中学习，那会更纯粹，而且最终也会把你带得更远。但如果你是想先走到 AGI，你就需要先借助现有 data 来 bootstrap（自举）自己，而这正是整个 language model（语言模型）革命向我们展示的东西：在很长一段时间里，其实几乎没有多少 reinforcement learning——如果你不把 reinforcement learning from human

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: if if their base models weren't weren't strong enough, right, except for these these specific domains.
And then, you know, what's fascinating, obviously, about this moment in time is the combination of large pre training LMs and then reinforcement learning on these on these verifiable domains.

paragraphs.zh: feedback 算进去的话——前提是它们的 base models（基础模型）本身还不够强，对吧，除了那些特定领域之外。然后，显然，这个时间点最迷人的地方就在于：large pre-training LMs（大规模预训练语言模型）与在这些可验证领域上的 reinforcement learning 的结合。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But it's

paragraphs.zh: 但这件事很有意思。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: just interesting that he that he left at this time because, obviously, it feels like, generally, reinforcement learning is back in vogue as, like, the way to improve, you know, these these models.
And so, you know, it almost would have made more sense a few years ago when when when reinforcement learning was more out of favor.
I thought it was interesting timing that that had happened now.

paragraphs.zh: 只是有意思的是，他偏偏在这个时候离开，因为很明显，总体感觉上，reinforcement learning（强化学习）又重新流行起来了，成了提升这些模型的路径。也就是说，如果是在几年前、也就是 reinforcement learning 更不受青睐的时候离开，反而几乎还更说得通一些。我只是觉得，这件事现在发生，这个时间点很耐人寻味。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
I mean, I think I think you're right, but it it sort of reflects the human lag between feeling something and acting on it.
Right?

I think he'd been feeling for a while frankly that he was swamped in a big organization which didn't fundamentally want to put more than a sort of small amount of its chips on the reinforcement learning table.
And then even within reinforcement learning, you know, his vision of how it should be done differed from some other people.

paragraphs.zh: 对。我觉得你说得对，但这某种程度上也反映了人从“有这种感受”到“真正采取行动”之间的滞后，对吧？坦白说，我觉得他已经有一段时间都在感觉，自己被困在一个大型组织里，忙得不可开交，而这个组织从根本上就不想把超过一小部分筹码押在 reinforcement learning 这张牌桌上。甚至即便是在 reinforcement learning 内部，他对于这件事应该怎么做的设想，也和其他一些人不太一样。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So I think he is kind of a classic startup sort of person who who wants to be in a small organization where his vision is the vision because, you know, he's he's extremely visionary on on reinforcement learning.
And so it makes sense for him to go do his own thing.

paragraphs.zh: 所以我觉得，他算是那种很典型的 startup 型人物，想待在一个小组织里，在那里他的愿景就是那个组织的愿景。因为你知道，他在 reinforcement learning 方面确实非常有远见。所以他出去自己做，很合理。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: One thing that we've kind of talked about a bunch here is just like in the end of the day, there's a few people here that that run these labs and have intensely, like, personal histories and and relationships.
And I think the obviously, the Sam Dario relationship has been incredibly well documented.
Elon, Sam, all coming out in this in this current court case.
You know, I think Demis' relationship with each of them is probably less well understood by, you know, by the general public.
And I'm wondering if you could just talk a little bit about that and his the extent to which those relationships exist, his kind of feelings on the two main other protagonists of space right now.

paragraphs.zh: 我们在这里其实反复聊到过的一点是，说到底，这些 lab（实验室）其实是由少数几个人在运作，而这些人之间都有非常强烈的个人经历和人际关系。我觉得，显然，Sam 和 Dario 之间的关系已经被大量报道了。Elon、Sam，这些也都在当前这场 court case（诉讼案）里被翻了出来。不过我想，Demis 和他们每个人之间的关系，普通公众可能反而没那么了解。我想请你稍微谈谈这一点：这些关系究竟在多大程度上存在，以及他对当下这个领域另外两位主要主角的大致看法。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Demis' relationship with Elon is very interesting.

paragraphs.zh: Demis 和 Elon 的关系非常有意思。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: You know, because tried to buy him.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 你知道的，因为他曾试图收购他，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
But it but just going back, I mean, you know, the the origin of this whole thing is that they were both funded by the same VC shop, founder's fund.
Right?
Where, you know, Elon was the SpaceX guy and Demis is the DeepMind guy.
They both in 2012 or thereabouts get invited to an LP off-site and they both present and then they get talking to each other.

paragraphs.zh: 对。但如果再往前说，你知道，整件事的起点其实是，他们两人都拿过同一家 VC（风险投资）机构 founder's fund 的钱，对吧？当时，Elon 是 SpaceX 的那个人，Demis 是 DeepMind 的那个人。然后在 2012 年前后，他们都被邀请去参加一次 LP 线下活动，之后两人都做了分享，然后就聊了起来。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And, you know, Elon is always very competitive.

paragraphs.zh: 而且，你知道，Elon 一向都非常好胜。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: That would have been a pretty valuable off-site to pay attention Too too pretty.

paragraphs.zh: 那本来会是一场非常值得关注的场外会谈——太有意思了，实在太精彩了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.

So Elon's like, you know, well, I've got the most important technology in the world because, you know, even if the world is screwed up by your AI, we can all move to Mars, be a multi planetary species.
So I've got the most important thing.
At which point, Dennis says, yeah, But if you think you're gonna be safe on Mars, remember that my AI will be able to conquer space flight and it will just follow you to Mars.
So then you won't be safe after all.

paragraphs.zh: 对。所以 Elon 就像是在说，你知道，我掌握着世界上最重要的 technology（技术），因为，你知道，就算这个世界被你的 AI（人工智能）搞砸了，我们也都可以搬去 Mars，成为一个跨行星物种。所以我掌握的是最重要的东西。然后 Dennis 就说，是啊，但如果你以为你在 Mars 上就会安全，别忘了，我的 AI 将能够征服 space flight（太空飞行），它会直接跟着你去 Mars。那样一来，你终究还是不安全。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And then there's a silence.
And then Elon goes, and then the next thing he he says, well, I'd like to invest in your series b.
And he writes a $5,000,000 check into the series b.
And then as you say, he wants to buy DeepMind, at the start of 2014 to prevent it from being sold to Google.
And and Demis just like waves them off.

paragraphs.zh: 然后现场沉默了一下。接着 Elon 说，而他接下来讲的是，好吧，我想投资你们的 series b（B 轮融资）。于是他在这轮 series b 里开出了一张 5,000,000 美元的支票。然后，正如你说的，2014 年初，他还想收购 DeepMind，以阻止它被卖给 Google。而 Demis 只是把他们挥手打发了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And there's this crazy story where Luke Nozick, the founder's fund partner who is sitting on SpaceX's board, you know, sees Elon at a party in LA and they agree that they've just gotta stop DeepMind from being sold to Larry Page.
He's a transhumanist.
You can't trust him.
So they go up to some closet upstairs in this party, and they Skype Demis in the middle of the night in London and say, you gotta sell to us, not to them.
And, you know, sell it to SpaceX.

paragraphs.zh: 还有个很疯狂的故事：Luke Nozick，那位 Founders Fund 的合伙人，同时也坐在 SpaceX 的董事会里，你知道，在 LA 的一个派对上碰到了 Elon，两人一致认为他们必须阻止 DeepMind 卖给 Larry Page。他是个 transhumanist（超人类主义者），你不能信任他。于是他们跑到这个派对楼上的某个储藏间里，半夜给身在 London 的 Demis 打 Skype，对他说，你得卖给我们，别卖给他们。你知道，卖给 SpaceX 吧。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Sell it to Tesla.
Do something, but just don't sell to Google because they're evil.
And Demis is like, know, no. No. Google's got the computer.

paragraphs.zh: 卖给 Tesla。随便做点什么都行，但就是别卖给 Google，因为他们是邪恶的。而 Demis 的反应是，你知道，不，不行。不。Google 才有 computer（计算资源）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I'm selling to them.
Goodbye.
Goodnight.
And he puts the phone down.
And then Elon goes nuts, right, and starts calling Demis an evil genius, which is a reference to a game that Demis worked on when he was a video game designer.

paragraphs.zh: 我要卖给他们。再见。晚安。然后他就把电话挂了。接着 Elon 就炸了，对吧，开始叫 Demis 是个 evil genius（邪恶天才）；这其实是在引用 Demis 以前当 video game designer（电子游戏设计师）时参与过的一款游戏。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And, you know, vilifies Demis and is obsessed with this.
But I think this has come out again a bit more in the trial that we've just been having recently, know, the the Elon versus Sam trial.
And the kind of obsession that Elon had with Demis as the evil genius that had to be counteracted.
So that's the history there.
Demis is kind of very keen to say that these days they get on fine.

paragraphs.zh: 然后，你知道，他不断妖魔化 Demis，还对这件事非常执着。不过我觉得，这一点最近我们刚经历的那场庭审里又更明显地浮现出来了，你知道，就是那场 Elon versus Sam 的庭审。还有 Elon 对 Demis 这种执念——把他视作一个必须被制衡的 evil genius。所以这就是那段历史。Demis 现在倒是很愿意强调，如今他们相处得挺好。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And I haven't heard that directly from Elon, but I kind of feel that probably water under the bridge, Elon has moved on to fighting with Sam.
It probably is true that he's okay with with Demis now.
But, you know, that's the history there.
And then with Sam, it's just such a complete difference in personality and background if you compare the two of them.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 我没有直接从 Elon 那里听到这件事，但我有点感觉，这大概已经翻篇了，Elon 现在已经转去和 Sam 对着干了。他现在多半确实已经能接受 Demis 了。不过，你知道，这就是其中的历史背景。至于 Sam，如果把他们两个人放在一起比较，那在性格和背景上简直是完全不同，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So Demis has a Nobel Prize.
Sam didn't finish his first degree.
Therefore, Demis doesn't take Sam very seriously.
Right?
He doesn't have a college degree, let alone a PhD or a Nobel Prize.

paragraphs.zh: Demis 拿过 Nobel Prize。Sam 连第一个学位都没读完。因此，Demis 并不把 Sam 看得很重，对吧？他连 college degree 都没有，更别说 PhD 或 Nobel Prize 了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And and he just, you know, to to Demis, Sam and I think there's some truth in this, frankly, that, you know, Sam is the sort of skillful ultimate embodiment of the Silicon Valley network who knows how to fake it until you make it, to tell the premature truth, who is just a master at raising money at leveraging his connections in the valley.
And, you know, that's all great, but, you know, it's not the same as being a serious scientist.
And you can't trust somebody like that.
And I think, you know, I think people have come around to Demis' view to quite some, you know, some extent with Sam.
But but Demis always felt that.

paragraphs.zh: 而且，你知道，在 Demis 看来——坦率地说，我觉得这里面也有几分道理——Sam 是那种 Silicon Valley 人脉网络最娴熟、最极致的化身，懂得 how to fake it until you make it，懂得说出 premature truth，极其擅长融资，也极其擅长利用自己在 the valley 的关系。你知道，这些当然都很厉害，但这跟成为一个严肃的科学家不是一回事。你也不能信任这样的人。我觉得，你知道，在相当程度上，人们后来也逐渐接受了 Demis 对 Sam 的这种看法。不过 Demis 一直都是这么想的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And so I don't think he ever liked Sam.

paragraphs.zh: 所以我觉得他从来就不喜欢 Sam。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I think it was The Economist that in talking about your book, you know, said it's really like a test of this, like, great man theory of history.
But I'm curious, we've talked a lot about these four protagonists.

And I'm wondering after having done all this work, was all of this inevitable or to what extent did it matter that it was you know, Demis and and maybe some of these other folks at the at the helm of the companies?
Yeah.
I'd be curious your thoughts on that.

paragraphs.zh: 我记得好像是 The Economist 在谈到你的书时说，这本书其实很像是在检验那种“历史伟人理论”。不过我很好奇，我们已经谈了很多关于这四位主角的事。我想问的是，在你做了这么多研究之后，这一切到底是不是不可避免的？或者说，由 Demis 以及也许其他这些人来掌舵这些公司，这件事究竟有多重要？对这个问题，我很想听听你的看法。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I once wrote a book about Alan Greenspan, a super powerful player in global economics, but ultimately unable to stop the financial bubble of two thousand eight.
And I called the book The Man Who Knew because he understood that bubbles were dangerous.
That's in fact what he read his PhD about.
He was obsessed with bubbles blowing up, but he couldn't stop this thing from happening.
And I feel that one could have almost used the same title for the Demis book.

paragraphs.zh: 我曾经写过一本关于 Alan Greenspan 的书。他是全球经济中一个权势极大的角色，但最终却没能阻止 2008 年的金融泡沫。我把那本书叫作 The Man Who Knew，因为他知道泡沫是危险的。事实上，这正是他 PhD 研究的内容。他一直痴迷于泡沫破裂这件事，但他还是无法阻止这一切发生。我觉得，关于 Demis 的这本书，几乎也可以用同样的标题。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Right?
I called it the infinity machine, but it could have been called the man who knew because Demis has known from the beginning that this thing is dangerous.
But as the leader of one lab, even a very powerful rich lab, even he with his stature as a Nobel Prize winner, he understands that it's dangerous but what can he do?
Because if he makes his own lab safe, it doesn't stop the other guys from being unsafe.
And so I think there is a sort of this inevitability to the race dynamic.

paragraphs.zh: 对吧？我把它叫作 The Infinity Machine，但它本来也可以叫作 The Man Who Knew，因为 Demis 从一开始就知道这东西是危险的。但是，作为一家 lab 的领导者——哪怕是一家非常强大、非常富有的 lab——即使是他，哪怕他有 Nobel Prize 得主这样的地位，他也明白这很危险，但他又能做什么呢？因为即便他让自己的 lab 变得安全，也无法阻止其他人继续不安全。所以我认为，这种竞赛动态里确实有某种不可避免性。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It does matter who is leading these labs.
I mean, clearly, you know, Dario changed the narrative on AI safety first by fighting with the Pentagon in public and secondly and more importantly by the way that he did the Mythos release.
So that's a clear example.
On the other hand, you know, Sam decided to release ChattyPT even though it was hallucinating.
That was a choice.

paragraphs.zh: 由谁来领导这些 lab，确实很重要。我的意思是，很明显，Dario 改变了关于 AI safety（AI 安全）的叙事：第一，他公开与 Pentagon 对抗；第二，而且更重要的是，他发布 Mythos 的方式。所以这是一个很清楚的例子。另一方面，你知道，Sam 决定发布 ChattyPT，尽管它当时还会 hallucinating（产生幻觉、一本正经地胡说八道）。那是一个选择。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It didn't have to do that And that completely colored the way the AI race played out.
And Demis, in a more understated way, told Rishi Sunak, The UK prime minister at the time, hey, we should have a global AI safety summit.
And, you know, that's what happened.
And there was one in Bletchley Park and the Chinese came and that was kind of the beginnings of international conversation on AI safety.
So I think Demis is more behind the scenes in what he does.
