paragraphs.zh: 其实他本不必那么做，而这也彻底影响了这场 AI 竞赛后来的展开方式。Demis 还以一种更克制、更低调的方式对当时的英国首相 Rishi Sunak 说：我们应该办一个全球 AI 安全峰会。后来这件事确实发生了，在 Bletchley Park 举办了一次，中国方面也来了，那某种程度上就是国际社会就 AI 安全展开对话的开端。所以我觉得，Demis 做事更多是在幕后发力。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But, yeah, I think it matters, the personalities of these leaders.
But it perhaps, you know, it's not the only thing that matters.
The there are underlying forces as well.

paragraphs.zh: 不过，是的，我认为这些领导者的个性确实重要。但也许，你知道，这并不是唯一重要的因素。背后还存在一些更深层的力量。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Well, before we wrap up, I definitely you know, given that you spent so much time with Dennis, I I think there's a few just questions about that process I was I was really curious to dig into.
You know, you spent, I guess, three years meeting him at the same pub.
And I'm wondering across those series of conversations, like, what was the moment, I guess, your view of him kind of shifted the most?

paragraphs.zh: 在我们收尾之前，鉴于你和 Dennis 相处了这么久，我确实还有几个关于那个过程的问题，挺想深挖一下。你大概连续三年都在同一家 pub 见他。我想知道，在那一系列对话中，哪一个时刻最让你对他的看法发生了变化？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: There were lots of surprises, but one of them is the depth of his conviction about discovering the deep mysteries of science.
And this is actually a kind of spiritual conviction, which I had no inkling of before.
But, you know, he would sometimes erupt in these conversations I was having.
They went on for two hours each time so we already could, you know, get deep on stuff.
And he would start banging the table and saying, look, this table, it's a mystery.

paragraphs.zh: 让我惊讶的地方有很多，其中之一是：他对于揭开科学深层奥秘这件事，怀有极其强烈的信念。实际上，这甚至是一种带有 spiritual（精神性/灵性）色彩的信念，而我此前对此毫无察觉。不过你知道，有时候在我们的谈话中，他会突然激动起来。我们每次都会聊两个小时，所以已经足够深入。然后他会开始拍桌子，说，你看，这张桌子，本身就是个谜。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It's a mystery.
Don't understand it.
Like, these atoms jumping around and there are gaps between them and yet the table is solid.
And why is your laptop able to think when it's just a bunch of sand and copper?
And and, you know, what's going on here?

paragraphs.zh: 这是个谜。我们并不理解它。比如，这些 atoms（原子）在四处运动，它们之间还有空隙，可桌子却是坚固的。还有，为什么你的 laptop 只是一堆 sand 和 copper，却居然能够思考？这背后到底发生了什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Why is the world set up like this so that it functions?
It's a mystery.
We have to understand.
There must be some sort of intelligence behind it.
This can't just be coincidence that it's like this.

paragraphs.zh: 为什么世界会被设定成这种能够运转的样子？这是个谜。我们必须理解它。其背后一定存在某种 intelligence（智能/智慧）。它不可能只是巧合，恰好就是这个样子。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Maybe maybe it's like God.
Maybe if we approach science the right way, we understand more about nature, we will be getting closer to something that we could perhaps call God.
Now I had no idea that he would feel that way, but I think he does.
And and it explains why he nonetheless pushes forward to, you know, develop this super strong AI, which he knows to be dangerous is because it's it's it's this kind of quasi spiritual quest for him.

paragraphs.zh: 也许——也许那就像 God。也许如果我们以正确的方式接近科学，更多地理解自然，我们就会更接近某种也许可以称之为 God 的东西。我以前完全不知道他会这样想，但我觉得他确实是这么想的。而这也解释了，为什么他明知这种超强 AI 很危险，却仍然要继续推进它的发展——因为这对他来说，是一种近乎 spiritual（精神性/灵性）的探索。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And it feels like it's it for all for a lot of people in the space, there's like a religious or spiritual element to to, like, getting to AGI.
And it it it's really interesting to see play out.
And, certainly, I thought that that came through really clearly in in in your work.
I guess, you know, it feels like you you had free you know, such a wide ranging and open book.
Were there things that Demis, like, wasn't really willing to talk about or or areas that you would have liked to put in the book but but but, you know, didn't end up coming through?

paragraphs.zh: 而且感觉在这个领域里，对很多人来说，通往 AGI 的过程里都带有某种宗教或 spiritual（精神性/灵性）元素。看着这种倾向真实地展开，确实很有意思。而且我当然觉得，这一点在你的作品里体现得非常清楚。我想问的是，感觉 Demis 在你面前已经相当开放、几乎无所不谈了；但是否还是有一些东西，是他真的不太愿意谈的，或者有一些你原本想写进书里、但最终没能呈现出来的内容？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
He was very clear at the start that he would talk a lot about himself, but he wouldn't talk about his family.
And so I left that out.
And there are passing references.
He's know, he married his girlfriend from Cambridge.

paragraphs.zh: 对。他一开始就说得很清楚：他会大量谈自己，但不会谈家人。所以我把那部分略去了。书里只有一些顺带提及。你知道的，他娶了他在 Cambridge 时交往的女友。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: They're still married.
They have two children.
It's all very normal.

But I basically don't talk I mean, most by when I wrote about Greenspan, going to speak to his many, many, many ex girlfriends was one way of understanding the guy and actually brought out the human side of him in a good way.
And I, you know, I didn't really go there with Demis.

paragraphs.zh: 他们现在还在婚姻中，有两个孩子，一切都很正常。但我基本上不谈这些。我的意思是，当年我写 Greenspan 时，去采访他那许许多多的前女友，是理解这个人的一种方式，而且确实很好地展现了他更有人味的一面。但写 Demis 时，我没有真的往那个方向去。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Another thing is, you know, he didn't wanna talk about fights between himself and Sundar Pichai and the leadership of Google and Mountain View.
I did write about that because other people told me about that and so I get into that quite a lot.
So you know his preference that this should be left out was not honored.
You know, he didn't really want me to talk about the way that he fired his co founder, Mustafa Soleiman.
And he would sort of say I didn't really fire him.

paragraphs.zh: 还有一件事是，你知道，他不想谈他和 Sundar Pichai 以及 Google、Mountain View 管理层之间的冲突。我还是写了，因为其他人跟我讲了这些，所以我在这方面写了不少。所以，他希望这些内容被略去的偏好并没有被遵从。你知道，他也不太想让我谈他是怎么解雇联合创始人 Mustafa Soleiman 的。而他会有点像是在说，我其实并没有真的解雇他。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You know there was a process, some sort of inquiry into bullying done by my general counsel and so forth.
And you know that was kind of the trigger but it wasn't the deep reason why Mustafa was pushed out.
Nothing happened at DeepMind unless Demis wanted it to happen.
And I think he just decided it was time for Mustafa to go because they disagreed on too many issues.
So yeah, there were some things that he didn't want included and sometimes he won that preference and other times he didn't.

paragraphs.zh: 你知道，当时有一个过程，某种针对霸凌问题的调查，由我的 general counsel（总法律顾问）之类的人来做，等等。你知道，那算是一个导火索，但并不是 Mustafa 被逼走的深层原因。DeepMind 里没有任何事会在 Demis 不想发生的情况下发生。我觉得他只是认定 Mustafa 该走了，因为他们在太多问题上意见不合。所以，是的，确实有些内容他不想被写进去；有时他的这种偏好奏效了，有时则没有。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: There's been a ton of public discourse about your book.
I feel like you've been on on you know, lots lots of folks have been talking about it, and I think you picked up on a lot of the themes we've discussed here today.
Are there parts of the book or or or aspects of it that you think are, like, under discussed or you wish people, you know, would would would talk about more?

paragraphs.zh: 你的书已经引发了大量公开讨论。我感觉你最近到处都在接受采访，很多很多人都在谈这本书，而且我觉得你抓住了我们今天在这里讨论的很多主题。你觉得这本书里有没有哪些部分，或者哪些侧面，是讨论得还不够的，或者你希望人们能更多谈一谈的？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I think there are sort of interesting takeaways for scientific innovation, how you do deep tech companies.
Know, You we talked about some of the shortcomings at DeepMind, you know, the lack of concentrated bets and and so forth.
But on the upside, I think it's very interesting how he developed what he calls scientific taste.

And what I mean here is that, you know, when he had his first startup, which was this video game production company called Elixir, he basically blew it up by being too ambitious in terms of the product engineering.
Like, how fantastic could the graphics be?

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得，这里面对于科学创新、以及如何经营 deep tech companies（深科技公司），有一些很有意思的启示。你知道，我们谈过 DeepMind 的一些缺点，比如缺乏集中的押注，诸如此类。但从积极的一面看，我觉得很有意思的是，他发展出了他所说的 scientific taste（科学品味）。我的意思是，当他做第一家 startup，也就是那家名为 Elixir 的电子游戏制作公司时，他基本上是因为在产品工程上过于雄心勃勃而把公司搞砸了。比如说，画面到底能做得多惊艳？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Could you do kind of early reinforcement learning to make the characters more interesting?
And he drove his technical team, like, over the brink in terms of how ambitious they had to be.
And they couldn't ship product on time as a result.
And so that was a bad outcome.
You know, he got some money out of that first company, but it wasn't as much of a success as he hoped.

paragraphs.zh: 能不能做某种早期的 reinforcement learning（强化学习），让角色变得更有趣？而他对技术团队的驱动，在雄心要求上几乎把他们逼到了极限。结果他们无法按时交付产品。所以这是个糟糕的结果。你知道，他从第一家公司里确实赚到了一些钱，但远没有达到他原本期待的成功程度。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But then you fast forward and he's doing DeepMind.
And he gets this moment in 2018 where, you know, he's two years into the AlphaFold research and his AlphaFold team has produced the best protein prediction system in the world.
And the boss of that team, Andrew Sr, says, okay, boss.
You know, we've okay, Demis.
We've done this now.

paragraphs.zh: 但再往后看，他开始做 DeepMind。到了 2018 年，他迎来了这样一个时刻：你知道，AlphaFold 的研究已经做了两年，而他的 AlphaFold 团队已经做出了世界上最好的蛋白质预测系统。那个团队的负责人 Andrew Sr 说，好了，老板。你知道，我们——好吧，Demis。我们现在已经做到这一步了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Let's declare victory and move on because we're not gonna predict proteins precisely.
Give me a break.
That's impossible.
We're the best in the world.
That's good enough.

paragraphs.zh: 我们就宣布胜利然后继续往前吧，因为我们不可能精确预测 proteins（蛋白质）。别闹了。这不可能。我们已经是世界上最好的了。这样就够好了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And Demis is like, no. No. That's not the point.
We we wanna predict proteins so that, you know, research biologists and and medical researchers can use our predictions to build medicines and other fantastic breakthroughs.
There's no point just being the best better than the other labs.

paragraphs.zh: 而 Demis 的反应是，不，不，这不是重点。我们的目标是预测 proteins（蛋白质），这样的话，research biologists（生物研究者）和 medical researchers（医学研究者）就能利用我们的预测来制造药物，以及实现其他了不起的突破。仅仅比其他 labs（实验室）更强，是没有意义的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: We wanna solve this problem.
And the guy who's running the team, Andrew Sr, says that's impossible.
So Demes sits in the meetings of the team and this is where the scientific taste come in.
He he listens to what he calls you know, he listens for the fluidity and the fluidity of ideas.
If the research team are, like, bouncing possibilities of stuff they could look into off of each other, then that's fluent, and and you should move forward.

paragraphs.zh: 我们是想真正解决这个问题。而当时带这个团队的人 Andrew Sr 说那是不可能的。于是 Demis 会坐进团队的会议里，这也正是 scientific taste（科学判断力）发挥作用的地方。他会去听他所说的那种“fluidity”，也就是想法流动的顺畅程度。如果 research team（研究团队）成员之间会彼此抛接各种他们可以探索的可能性，那就说明这种流动性是存在的，而你就应该继续推进。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Put more resources in.
Push harder.
If there was kind of a silence and nobody had any good ideas, of course, you should give up.
And so he he does that test.
He listens to the team.

paragraphs.zh: 投入更多资源。更用力地往前推。相反，如果现场某种程度上陷入沉默，谁也提不出什么好点子，那当然就该放弃。所以他就用这个标准来判断。他倾听团队。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: They are fluid in their exchange of ideas.
And so then he basically switches out the project lead.
Andrew Sr. Does something else.
He puts in this other guy, John Jumper, who then goes on to become the co winner of the Nobel Prize with him.

paragraphs.zh: 他们在交换想法时是流畅的。于是他基本上把 project lead（项目负责人）换掉了。Andrew Sr 去做别的事了。他换上了另一个人，John Jumper，后来此人和他一起成为 Nobel Prize（诺贝尔奖）共同得主。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And I think that evolution from blowing up his first startup to being right about protein folding holds a lesson for how you do this sort of frontier science inside a company.

paragraphs.zh: 我认为，从他第一家 startup（创业公司）失败，到后来在 protein folding（蛋白质折叠）这件事上判断正确，这种演变过程给出了一个启示：在公司内部做这类 frontier science（前沿科学）应该怎么做。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I love that story.
I guess, know, obviously, you know, part of of of this work really helps elevate, you know, and and tell the story of Demis more broadly, which may be an under known story.

Throughout this work or in general, are there other folks in the AI ecosystem that you feel similarly deserve more light shown on them or an expose, some kind of biography, other undersung heroes in this kind of AI world?

paragraphs.zh: 我很喜欢这个故事。我想，显然，你知道，这项工作的一个重要作用，确实是帮助提升 Demis 的形象，也更广泛地讲述他的故事，而这可能是一个还没有被足够了解的故事。在这项工作中，或者更广义地说，在 AI ecosystem（AI 生态）里，还有没有其他人也值得你认为被投以更多关注，或者值得有一篇深度揭示、某种 biography（传记）来书写——也就是这个 AI 世界里其他那些被低估的英雄？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Well, in my book, do have this sort of pairing of Ilya Satskeva and David Silva, one of them representing the deep learning tradition, PhD under Geoffrey Hinton in Toronto.
And then on the other hand, David Silver representing what I call the kind of Edmonton, Alberta tradition, PhD under Rich Sutton who was the sort of guru of reinforcement learning.

And I think both of those individuals and I have kind of embedded in my story about Demis a kind of mini biography of the two of them kind of juxtaposing their two approaches, and there could be a fantastic double biography of the two of them.

paragraphs.zh: 嗯，在我的书里，我确实写到了一种对照式的人物搭配：Ilya Satskeva 和 David Silva，其中一位代表 deep learning（深度学习）传统，是 Geoffrey Hinton 在 Toronto 门下的 PhD；而另一边，David Silver 代表的是我所说的那种 Edmonton, Alberta 传统，是 Rich Sutton 门下的 PhD，而 Rich Sutton 可以说是 reinforcement learning（强化学习）领域的一位宗师。我认为这两个人都很值得关注，而且我也已经在关于 Demis 的叙事中嵌入了两人的“迷你传记”，把他们的两种路径并置起来；如果有人为他们两人写一本双人传记，那会非常精彩。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Certainly.
Know, I think at various times as as Google has AI fortunes have seemed better or worse, you know, folks have speculated that maybe one day Demis will become CEO of Google.
You think that's possible?

paragraphs.zh: 当然。我想，你知道，在不同时期，随着 Google 的 AI 运势看起来时好时坏，人们一直在猜测，也许有一天 Demis 会成为 Google 的 CEO。你觉得这有可能吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
It's possible.
There's a question about whether he would want to do that because I think he does love being a little bit able to think about the science.
And he does this double shift every day meaning, you know, he eats dinner with his family and then goes back to his desk until 4AM and that's when he's doing science.

Now I think actually these days a lot of that late time is spent doing calls to Mountain View and trying to coordinate with all, you know, all of the many people who report to him out of Mountain View.

paragraphs.zh: 是的，有可能。问题在于他是否想做这件事，因为我觉得他确实很喜欢还能留出一点时间思考科学。他现在每天都在上“双班”——也就是说，你知道，他会和家人一起吃晚饭，然后回到书桌前一直工作到凌晨 4 点，而那段时间就是他做科学的时候。不过我想，实际上这些天很多这样的深夜时间，都花在和 Mountain View 打电话、以及设法协调所有——你知道——那些在 Mountain View 向他汇报的许多人身上了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So already that science time is being eaten into, but if he became CEO, obviously, there'd be nothing left of it at all.
And I think he's genuinely conflicted about whether he won't want that.
But it seems to me that it it all depends kind of like if Sundar were to decide that he's leaving, you know, who would be the likely CEO replacement if it wasn't Demis?
And if it was somebody that Demis felt very comfortable with, he'd probably be perfectly happy not to do that.
If he was not comfortable with it, then he might kind of push his hat into the ring because he wouldn't he wouldn't want to work for somebody that he didn't agree with.

paragraphs.zh: 所以他那段做科学的时间其实已经在被侵蚀了；但如果他成了 CEO，显然那就一点都不剩了。我觉得他对自己是否会想要那个职位，内心是真的很矛盾。但在我看来，这一切某种程度上都取决于：如果 Sundar 决定离开，那么如果不是 Demis，最有可能接任 CEO 的会是谁？如果那个人是 Demis 非常放心的人，那他大概会完全乐意不去做这件事。如果他对那个人不放心，那他可能就会选择下场竞争，因为他不会想为一个自己理念不一致的人工作。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Well, Sebastian, it's been such an interesting conversation.
I always like to leave the last word to the guest.
I I think in this case, it's pretty clear where you might wanna point people, but, I'll leave the last word to you.
Anything our listeners should should take away further from this conversation, and and, also, please please plug the book.

paragraphs.zh: 好，Sebastian，这真是一场非常有意思的对话。我总是喜欢把最后的话留给嘉宾。我想在这个案例里，你可能会把大家引向哪里，其实已经很清楚了，不过最后这段还是交给你。对于我们的听众来说，还有什么是他们应该进一步从这场对话中带走的吗？还有，也请你顺便介绍一下这本书。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Well, look, I really enjoyed writing this book.
It's my sixth book and spending, you know, more than thirty hours at Demis de Sabes in the top of a British pub was really quite special.

He would like me to point out that we were drinking coffee, not pints of beer, But just simply the intellectual range of this guy who can riff about neuroscience, computer science, physics, biology, chemistry, you know, the history of movies, novels, science fiction.
It it was just wild.
And I do try to communicate that energy on the page.

paragraphs.zh: 你看，我真的很享受写这本书。这是我的第六本书，而在一间 British pub 的楼上和 Demis de Sabes 待了三十多个小时，真的是非常特别的经历。他会希望我说明一点：我们喝的是咖啡，不是一品脱啤酒。但这个人的智识跨度之大，真的令人惊叹——他可以滔滔不绝地谈 neuroscience（神经科学）、computer science（计算机科学）、physics、biology、chemistry，你知道，还有电影史、小说、science fiction（科幻）。那种感觉真的太疯狂了。而我也确实努力把那种能量传达在书页上。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It's the first time I've ever used the the first person as a device in the book because I wanted to use that dialogue I had with him.
And so having Demis Riffer than me kind of ask him a question or just you know, in that interspersed in what he's saying, this kind of what I'm thinking to help the reader sort of understand it.
I was this was such an extraordinary experience that I was driven to innovate the craft of writing in terms of my own craft.
So, yeah, I enjoyed it.
I hope you do too if you read it, everybody out there.

paragraphs.zh: 这是我第一次在书里使用第一人称作为一种写作手法，因为我想把我和他的那种对话感放进去。所以就是让 Demis 比我更像即兴发挥者，由我向他提问，或者说——你知道——把我在听他说话时穿插其间的那些想法写进去，帮助读者去理解。这次经历太不同寻常了，以至于我被推动着去在写作技法上做创新，就我自己的写作 craft 而言。所以，是的，我很享受这个过程。也希望如果你们读这本书，你们也会喜欢，各位。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Amazing.
Well, thanks so much for coming on the podcast to talk about it.

paragraphs.zh: 太棒了。非常感谢你来上这期 podcast，和我们聊这件事。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Fantastic.
Thank you, Jacob.
It's great.

paragraphs.zh: 太好了。谢谢你，Jacob。非常棒。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I'm Jacob Efron, and this has been Unsupervised Learning, a podcast where I get to talk to the smartest people in AI and ask them tons of questions about what's happening with models and what it means for businesses in the world.
As I hope is clear, I have a ton of fun doing this.
It's a nights and weekends project in addition to my day job as an investor at Redpoint.
But our ability to get these incredible guests on really comes from folks like you subscribing to the podcast, sharing it with friends.
It's really what ultimately makes this whole thing work.

paragraphs.zh: 我是 Jacob Efron，这里是 Unsupervised Learning，一档 podcast，在这里我可以和 AI 领域最聪明的一些人交流，向他们提出大量问题，聊聊 models 正在发生什么，以及这对现实世界中的企业意味着什么。希望大家已经能感受到，我做这件事真的非常开心。除了我在 Redpoint 担任投资人的本职工作之外，这还是一个利用夜晚和周末时间来做的项目。但我们之所以能请到这些不可思议的嘉宾，真正离不开像你这样的听众订阅这档 podcast，并把它分享给朋友。归根结底，正是这些支持让整件事得以运转。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And so please consider doing that, and thank you so much for your support and listening.
We'll see you next episode.

paragraphs.zh: 所以也请你考虑这样做，非常感谢你的支持和收听。我们下期节目再见。
